Authors: Keefer DK
Title: Landslides caused by earthquakes
Parameters: landslide area; earthquake magnitude; slope angle; lithology
Key_Findings: Established magnitude thresholds for earthquake-triggered landslides: M4 for rock falls, M5 for disrupted slides, M6 for coherent slides; catalogued 40 historical earthquakes showing landslide distances scale with magnitude up to 500 km for M9.2

Authors: Keefer DK
Title: The importance of earthquake-induced landslides to long-term slope erosion and slope-failure hazards in seismically active regions
Parameters: landslide erosion rate; recurrence interval; sediment volume
Key_Findings: Coseismic landslides contribute 2-58% of total long-term hillslope erosion in seismically active regions; earthquake-induced erosion is episodic but cumulatively dominant in areas with recurrence intervals shorter than ~1000 years

Authors: Dadson SJ, Hovius N, Chen H et al.
Title: Links between erosion, runoff variability and seismicity in the Taiwan orogen
Parameters: suspended sediment concentration; runoff; seismicity rate; denudation rate
Key_Findings: Mean erosion rate across Taiwan is 3.9-6.0 mm/yr with spatial variation controlled by runoff variability and seismicity; earthquake-prone regions show 2-3x higher erosion than aseismic areas with similar precipitation

Authors: Dadson SJ, Hovius N, Chen H et al.
Title: Earthquake-triggered increase in sediment delivery from an active mountain belt
Parameters: suspended sediment load; landslide inventory; river discharge
Key_Findings: The 1999 Chi-Chi earthquake triggered >20000 landslides that increased catchment-scale sediment concentrations by a factor of 5.3 during subsequent typhoon Toraji; suspended sediment yields remained elevated for at least 4 years post-earthquake

Authors: Korup O, McSaveney MJ, Davies TRH
Title: Sediment generation and delivery from large historic landslides in the Southern Alps, New Zealand
Parameters: landslide volume; sediment delivery ratio; channel aggradation depth
Key_Findings: Large rock avalanches in the Southern Alps deliver only 1-10% of their volume to the fluvial network within decades; most debris remains stored on hillslopes or as valley fills acting as long-term sediment capacitors

Authors: Korup O, Strom AL, Weidinger JT
Title: Fluvial response to large rock-slope failures: Examples from the Himalayas, the Tien Shan, and the Southern Alps in New Zealand
Region: Himalayas; Tien Shan; Southern Alps, New Zealand
Parameters: channel gradient; dam lake volume; knickpoint retreat rate; aggradation depth
Key_Findings: Rivers upstream of large rock avalanche dams aggrade by 10-100 m while downstream channels incise; knickpoints generated by dam breaching propagate upstream at rates of 0.1-1 km/yr creating persistent geomorphic signatures lasting centuries

Authors: Koi T, Hotta N, Ishigaki I et al.
Title: Prolonged impact of earthquake-induced landslides on sediment yield in a mountain watershed: The Tanzawa region, Japan
Parameters: sediment yield; landslide area; rainfall intensity; recovery time
Key_Findings: Sediment yield remained elevated for >30 years following the 1923 Kanto earthquake (M7.9) in Tanzawa watersheds; recovery half-life was approximately 10 years with complete return to background rates requiring several decades

Authors: Chuang SC, Chen H, Lin GW et al.
Title: Increase in basin sediment yield from landslides in storms following major seismic disturbance
Parameters: basin sediment yield; storm rainfall; landslide density; turbidity
Key_Findings: Post-Chi-Chi typhoon-driven sediment yields increased 4-8 fold compared to pre-earthquake storms of similar magnitude; the sediment mobilization efficiency per unit rainfall was persistently elevated indicating earthquake preconditioning of hillslopes

Authors: Chen CY
Title: Sedimentary impacts from landslides in the Tachia River Basin, Taiwan
Parameters: channel cross-section change; landslide dam volume; reservoir sedimentation rate
Key_Findings: Chi-Chi earthquake-triggered landslides deposited ~100 million m3 of sediment in the Tachia River system; Techi Reservoir sedimentation rate increased by a factor of 3 post-earthquake with coarse debris pulses propagating 5-15 km downstream

Authors: Yanites BJ, Tucker GE, Mueller KJ, Chen Y
Title: How rivers react to large earthquakes: evidence from central Taiwan
Parameters: channel width; incision rate; bedrock exposure; sediment cover thickness
Key_Findings: Rivers in the Chi-Chi earthquake hanging wall showed initial 2-4 m aggradation followed by rapid incision exceeding pre-earthquake rates within 5 years; channel narrowing of 20-40% accompanied incision as rivers adjusted to excess sediment supply

Authors: Korup O, Densmore AL, Schlunegger F
Title: The role of landslides in mountain range evolution
Parameters: landslide volume-frequency; orogen sediment budget; relief evolution
Key_Findings: Large earthquake-triggered landslides can reset topographic relief by tens of meters in a single event; over millennial timescales they dominate the erosion budget of active orogens contributing 50-80% of total denudation in ranges like the Southern Alps and Himalayas

Authors: Hovius N, Meunier P, Lin CW et al.
Title: Prolonged seismically induced erosion and the mass balance of a large earthquake
Parameters: mass wasting rate; landslide inventory; Choshui River sediment load; recovery time
Key_Findings: Post-Chi-Chi mass wasting peaked at >5x background rates and returned to pre-earthquake levels in ~6 years; the Choshui River removed >30% of coseismically added rock mass during that period driven by typhoon-triggered remobilization

Authors: Parker RN, Densmore AL, Rosser NJ et al.
Title: Mass wasting triggered by the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake is greater than orogenic growth
Parameters: landslide volume; coseismic uplift volume; erosion-uplift mass balance
Key_Findings: Total volume of landslides triggered by the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake (2.8 +/- 0.9 km3) exceeded the rock volume added by coseismic uplift by a factor of 4-9; single large earthquakes can thus cause net topographic lowering of the orogen

Authors: Shou KJ, Wu CC, Fei LY et al.
Title: Dynamic environment in the Ta-Chia River watershed after the 1999 Taiwan Chi-Chi earthquake
Parameters: channel bed elevation; riverbank erosion rate; landslide dam evolution; debris flow frequency
Key_Findings: The Jiufenershan landslide dam in Ta-Chia River created a 4 km backwater zone and 20 m channel bed rise; dam breaching generated a 10 m incision wave propagating downstream at ~1 km/yr with persistent channel instability a decade later

Authors: Steer P, Simoes M, Cattin R, Shyu JBH
Title: Erosion influences the seismicity of active thrust faults
Parameters: erosion rate; Coulomb stress change; seismicity rate; fault loading
Key_Findings: Typhoon Morakot-scale erosion events (~1 km3 removed in days) unload faults sufficiently to alter Coulomb stress by 0.1-1 MPa and modulate shallow seismicity rates; erosion-seismicity coupling creates a negative feedback on earthquake-driven topography

Authors: Huang RQ, Li WL
Title: Post-earthquake landsliding and long-term impacts in the Wenchuan earthquake area, China
Parameters: post-seismic landslide count; debris flow frequency; rainfall threshold
Key_Findings: Post-seismic landslide activity in Wenchuan increased 5-10 fold over 5 years with rainfall triggering thresholds reduced by 30-50% compared to pre-earthquake conditions; debris flows occurred at rainfall intensities as low as 10 mm/hr due to abundant loose material

Authors: Marc O, Hovius N, Meunier P
Title: Transient changes of landslide rates after earthquakes
Region: Chi-Chi Taiwan; Wenchuan China; Northridge USA
Parameters: landslide rate; recovery time; landslide size distribution; earthquake magnitude
Key_Findings: Post-seismic landslide rates decay exponentially with characteristic times of 1-4 years depending on earthquake magnitude and climate; recovery time scales with earthquake magnitude and is shortened by intense monsoon precipitation that rapidly evacuates unstable material

Authors: Liu F, Yang S
Title: The effect of the Wenchuan earthquake on the fluvial morphology in the Longmen Shan, eastern Tibetan Plateau
Parameters: channel width; knickpoint location; bed grain size; longitudinal profile
Key_Findings: Rivers in the Wenchuan rupture zone showed 50-200% channel widening and bed coarsening within 3 years; knickpoints formed at tributary junctions where landslide debris created local base-level rises of 5-15 m

Authors: Wang J, Jin Z, Hilton RG et al.
Title: Controls on fluvial evacuation of sediment from earthquake-triggered landslides
Parameters: suspended sediment load; landslide connectivity index; catchment area; discharge
Key_Findings: Only 38% of Wenchuan landslides directly connect to the fluvial network; connected landslides dominate the post-seismic sediment flux contributing >80% of elevated suspended loads while disconnected deposits remain as hillslope storage for decades

Authors: Marc O, Hovius N, Meunier P, Gorum T, Uchida T
Title: A seismologically consistent expression for the total area and volume of earthquake-triggered landsliding
Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Earth Surface
Parameters: total landslide area; total landslide volume; seismic moment; PGA
Key_Findings: Total landslide volume scales as a power law with seismic moment (exponent ~0.67) providing the first physics-based prediction of coseismic sediment generation; landslide area saturates for very large earthquakes as available slopes become exhausted

Authors: Marc O, Hovius N, Meunier P
Title: The mass balance of earthquakes and earthquake sequences
Parameters: coseismic uplift volume; landslide volume; net mass change; earthquake magnitude
Key_Findings: Net coseismic mass balance transitions from positive (uplift > erosion) below Mw ~7.5 to negative above; shallow thrust earthquakes in steep terrain produce the most disproportionate erosion creating a seismic topographic ceiling

Authors: Li GK, West AJ, Densmore AL et al.
Title: Connectivity of earthquake-triggered landslides with the fluvial network: Implications for landslide sediment transport after the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake
Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Earth Surface
Parameters: landslide-channel connectivity; sediment delivery ratio; channel distance; slope gradient
Key_Findings: Landslide connectivity to channels in Wenchuan is controlled by hillslope gradient and distance to channel: 85% of landslides within 100 m of channels are connected versus <20% beyond 500 m; connectivity determines whether sediment evacuates in years or persists for decades

Authors: Robinson TR, Davies TRH, Wilson TM, Orchiston C
Title: Coseismic landsliding estimates for an Alpine Fault earthquake and the consequences for erosion of the Southern Alps, New Zealand
Parameters: predicted landslide volume; river aggradation depth; sediment delivery; dam formation probability
Key_Findings: An Mw8 Alpine Fault earthquake would trigger 50000-100000 landslides generating 1-3 km3 of debris; rivers would aggrade 5-20 m across the western Southern Alps with complete sediment evacuation requiring 50-100 years

Authors: Croissant T, Lague D, Steer P, Davy P
Title: Rapid post-seismic landslide evacuation boosted by dynamic river width
Parameters: river width; sediment transport capacity; evacuation time; aggradation thickness
Key_Findings: Dynamic river widening in response to sediment overloading increases transport capacity by 3-10x compared to fixed-width models; this mechanism reduces post-seismic sediment evacuation timescales from centuries to decades explaining observed rapid geomorphic recovery

Authors: Wang W, Godard V, Liu-Zeng J et al.
Title: Perturbation of fluvial sediment fluxes following the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake
Parameters: suspended sediment flux; 10Be concentration; rainfall-sediment relationship
Key_Findings: Post-Wenchuan sediment fluxes show a 3-5 fold increase that is not proportional across grain sizes; 10Be dilution by shallow landslide material indicates the excess sediment is predominantly from coseismic deposits rather than new deep-seated failures

Authors: Fan XM, Domènech G, Scaringi G et al.
Title: Spatio-temporal evolution of mass wasting after the 2008 Mw 7.9 Wenchuan earthquake revealed by a detailed multi-temporal inventory
Parameters: landslide count; area change; NDVI recovery; temporal frequency
Key_Findings: Post-seismic landslide activity in Wenchuan peaked in 2008-2010 then declined with NDVI-based vegetation recovery reaching 60% within 5 years; however spatially heterogeneous patterns showed persistent hotspots along fault-adjacent valleys where shattered rock continued failing

Authors: Fan XM, Juang CH, Wasowski J et al.
Title: What we have learned from the 2008 Wenchuan Earthquake and its aftermath: A decade of research and challenges
Parameters: landslide inventory completeness; hazard chain documentation; monitoring technology evolution
Key_Findings: A decade of Wenchuan research revealed that earthquake hazard chains (landslide-debris flow-river damming-outburst flood) persist far longer than expected; post-seismic debris flows increased in frequency but decreased in magnitude over 10 years as the available loose material was gradually exhausted

Authors: Dingle EH, Sinclair HD, Attal M et al.
Title: Temporal variability in detrital 10Be concentrations in a large Himalayan catchment
Parameters: 10Be concentration; grain size; monsoon discharge; sediment provenance
Key_Findings: Detrital 10Be in the Marsyandi varies by a factor of 5 between monsoon and dry seasons due to mixing of deeply eroded landslide material with shallow soil; this temporal variability means single 10Be samples can misestimate millennial erosion rates by 2-3x in seismically active catchments

Authors: Croissant T, Steer P, Lague D et al.
Title: Seismic cycles, earthquakes, landslides and sediment fluxes: Linking tectonics to surface processes using a reduced-complexity model
Parameters: seismic cycle period; landslide volume; sediment flux; fluvial evacuation time
Key_Findings: Post-seismic sediment flux is connectivity-limited in steep catchments (evacuation < seismic cycle) but transport-limited in gentle catchments (evacuation > seismic cycle); this dichotomy controls whether landscapes reach steady-state between earthquakes or accumulate a sediment deficit

Authors: Fan XM, Scaringi G, Korup O et al.
Title: Earthquake-Induced Chains of Geologic Hazards: Patterns, Mechanisms, and Impacts
Parameters: hazard chain typology; cascade probability; temporal evolution; impact assessment
Key_Findings: Earthquake hazard chains follow a characteristic temporal cascade: coseismic landslides (seconds) to debris flows (days-years) to river aggradation (years-decades) to flood risk amplification (decades); each stage has distinct triggering thresholds that evolve as the landscape heals

Authors: Li G, Densmore AL, West AJ
Title: Competing Effects of Mountain Uplift and Landslide Erosion Over Earthquake Cycles
Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
Parameters: uplift-erosion ratio; mean topography; seismic cycle duration; landslide volume scaling
Key_Findings: Modeled orogen topography oscillates over seismic cycles with coseismic erosion lowering peaks by 5-50 m per event; steady-state mountain height is set by the competition between interseismic uplift rate and the coseismic erosion efficiency which itself depends on rock strength and slope gradient

Authors: Marc O, Behling R, Andermann C et al.
Title: Long-term erosion of the Nepal Himalayas by bedrock landsliding: the role of monsoons, earthquakes and giant landslides
Parameters: landslide area-frequency; erosion rate; monsoon triggering; giant landslide contribution
Key_Findings: Giant landslides (>1 km2) contribute 30-50% of total landslide erosion volume in Nepal despite being <1% of all events; earthquake triggering shifts the landslide size distribution toward larger failures increasing the geomorphic impact per event by an order of magnitude

Authors: Hovius N, Stark CP, Tutton MA, Abbott LD
Title: Landslide-driven drainage network evolution in a pre-steady-state mountain belt: Finisterre Mountains, Papua New Guinea
Parameters: drainage density; landslide rate; channel head migration; relief evolution
Key_Findings: Earthquake-triggered landslides drive drainage network expansion in rapidly uplifting terrain by creating new channel heads at landslide scars; drainage density increases by 15-30% after large seismic events fundamentally reorganizing the catchment geometry

Authors: Campforts B, Shobe CM, Steer P et al.
Title: HyLands 1.0: a hybrid landscape evolution model to simulate the impact of landslides and landslide-derived sediment on landscape evolution
Parameters: landslide probability; sediment thickness; channel bed evolution; landscape response time
Key_Findings: HyLands model shows that landslide-derived sediment creates transient fluvial aggradation up to 10x background that takes 10-100x longer to evacuate than the interseismic period; neglecting landslide sediment in landscape evolution models underestimates valley fill thickness by 50-80%

Authors: Hovius N, Meunier P, Lin CW et al.
Title: Earthquake statistics changed by typhoon-driven erosion
Parameters: seismicity rate; erosion volume; Coulomb stress; typhoon rainfall
Key_Findings: Typhoon Morakot removed ~1.2 km3 of material from southern Taiwan in 2009 causing a measurable decrease in shallow seismicity rates in the following 2 years; this demonstrates a direct coupling where surface erosion modulates crustal stress and earthquake occurrence

Authors: Izumiyama H, Uchida T, Horie K, Sakurai W
Title: Characteristics of sediment dynamics following large-scale sediment supply events in mountain watersheds in Japan
Parameters: sediment yield; debris flow volume; channel storage; event magnitude
Key_Findings: Japanese mountain watersheds show a characteristic two-phase sediment response: initial 3-5 year pulse of debris flows mobilizing channel-stored material followed by a 10-30 year tail of elevated fluvial transport; total evacuation efficiency ranges from 20-60% depending on catchment gradient and rainfall regime

Authors: Kincey ME, Rosser NJ, Robinson TR et al.
Title: Evolution of Coseismic and Post-seismic Landsliding After the 2015 Mw 7.8 Gorkha Earthquake, Nepal
Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Earth Surface
Parameters: landslide count; new vs reactivated ratio; NDVI recovery; monsoon correlation
Key_Findings: Post-Gorkha landslide rates remained 3-5x above pre-earthquake background for at least 5 years; 60% of post-seismic landslides were reactivations of coseismic scars rather than new failures indicating persistent slope damage controls the post-seismic hazard trajectory

Authors: Croissant T, Hilton RG, Li GK et al.
Title: Pulsed carbon export from mountains by earthquake-triggered landslides explored in a reduced-complexity model
Parameters: organic carbon flux; landslide-derived POC; oxidation rate; fluvial transport time
Key_Findings: Over 90% of landslide-mobilized particulate organic carbon is exported from mountain catchments within 1-2 seismic cycles; rapid fluvial evacuation limits in-transit oxidation meaning earthquakes create pulsed carbon transfers from rock to ocean on decadal timescales

Authors: Tanyaş H, Kirschbaum D, Görüm T et al.
Title: A closer look at factors governing landslide recovery time in post-seismic periods
Parameters: landslide recovery time; NDVI; rainfall; earthquake magnitude; lithology
Key_Findings: Landslide recovery time shows a non-linear dependence on earthquake magnitude with Mw7+ events requiring 5-10 years for landslide rates to return to background; wet tropical climates recover 2-3x faster than arid regions due to accelerated vegetation regrowth and sediment flushing

Authors: Xu Q, Li WL, Dong XJ et al.
Title: Catastrophic debris flows triggered by the 20 August 2019 rainfall, a decade since the Wenchuan earthquake, China
Parameters: debris flow volume; rainfall intensity-duration; source material; fatalities
Key_Findings: Catastrophic debris flows in 2019 (11 years post-earthquake) killed 13 people showing coseismic sediment legacy persists beyond expected recovery; the debris flows sourced from reactivated coseismic landslide deposits suggesting that single extreme rainfall events can reset the sediment evacuation clock

Authors: Francis OR, Fan XM, Hales TC et al.
Title: The Fate of Sediment After a Large Earthquake
Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Earth Surface
Parameters: sediment cascade model; landslide-to-channel transfer; debris flow initiation; fluvial export
Key_Findings: A sediment cascade model for Wenchuan shows that only 5-15% of coseismic landslide volume reaches the main river within the first decade; debris flows are the primary transfer mechanism in steep tributaries while fluvial transport dominates in mainstem channels creating a spatially variable evacuation pattern

Authors: Blagen JR, Davies TR, Wells A, Norton D
Title: Post-seismic aggradation history of the West Coast, South Island, Aotearoa/New Zealand; dendrogeomorphological evidence and disaster recovery implications
Region: West Coast, South Island, New Zealand
Parameters: tree ring dating; aggradation depth; terrace age; floodplain evolution
Key_Findings: Dendrogeomorphology reveals 5-15 m of post-seismic aggradation on West Coast rivers following Alpine Fault earthquakes in 1717 AD and earlier; recovery to pre-earthquake channel geometry took 100-200 years indicating that major fault earthquakes create centuries-long fluvial disturbance cycles

Authors: Tang C, Van Westen CJ, Tanyas H, Jetten VG
Title: Investigation of the 2019 Wenchuan County debris flow disaster suggests nonuniform spatial and temporal post-seismic debris flow evolution patterns
Parameters: debris flow occurrence; spatial pattern; temporal evolution; sediment recharge ratio
Key_Findings: Debris flow activity in Wenchuan shows highly nonuniform spatial evolution: valleys directly on the rupture zone showed declining activity by 2019 while adjacent valleys with large sediment stores showed increasing activity; this spatial heterogeneity invalidates simple exponential decay models for post-seismic hazard

Authors: Tunnicliffe J, Howarth J, Massey C
Title: Controls on fluvial sediment evacuation following an earthquake-triggered landslide: Observations from LiDAR time series
Parameters: LiDAR DEM differencing; sediment evacuation rate; channel morphology change; debris volume
Key_Findings: 13 repeat LiDAR surveys over 5 years tracked 6.6 million m3 of landslide debris in the Hapuku River; 25% was evacuated in the first 2 years with export rate declining exponentially; debris fan progradation created a 2 km alluvial wedge that stores sediment for decades

Authors: Graf ELS, Sinclair HD, Attal M et al.
Title: Geomorphological and hydrological controls on sediment export in earthquake-affected catchments in the Nepal Himalaya
Parameters: suspended sediment yield; catchment area; monsoon discharge; landslide density
Key_Findings: Post-Gorkha earthquake sediment export varies by 2 orders of magnitude between catchments with similar earthquake damage; catchment hypsometry and monsoon runoff intensity are stronger controls on evacuation rate than landslide density alone challenging the assumption that coseismic damage predicts post-seismic sediment flux

Authors: Ruetenik GA, Ferrier KL, Marc O
Title: Decadal-scale decay of landslide-derived fluvial suspended sediment after Typhoon Morakot
Parameters: suspended sediment concentration; typhoon frequency; recovery half-life; sediment rating curves
Key_Findings: Suspended sediment concentrations in southern Taiwan rivers showed a clear exponential decay over 10+ years following Typhoon Morakot with a half-life of 3-5 years; recovery timescales are longer in catchments with deeper landslide scars and less frequent subsequent typhoons

Authors: Li GK, West AJ, Jin Z et al.
Title: Large riverbed sediment flux sustained for a decade after an earthquake
Parameters: bedload flux; gravel tracer; channel bed surveys; 10Be concentrations
Key_Findings: Direct bedload measurements in the Min Jiang River show that 9% of coseismic landslide volume was exported as coarse bedload over 10 years post-Wenchuan earthquake; bedload flux remained 5-10x above pre-earthquake levels with no sign of declining creating a sustained coarse sediment pulse that dominates channel morphology

Authors: O. Koltun
Title: The geomorphic features of the landslide on Kuprin St. in Khmelnytskyi City
Journal: Visnyk of the Lviv University. Series Geography
Parameters: sediment yield/flux
Key_Findings: The landslide in Quaternary loess sediments occurs a balka at the Samets River left bank and is limited by Metropolitan Sheptytsky St.

Authors: M. López‐Vicente, J. Poesen, A. Navas, et al.
Title: Predicting runoff and sediment connectivity and soil erosion by water for different land use scenarios in the Spanish Pre-Pyrenees
Parameters: sediment yield/flux
Key_Findings: Abstract Overland flow connectivity and runoff and sediment trap effectiveness are currently the cutting edge topics in soil erosion research.

Authors: A. Grande, A. Schmidt, P. Bierman, et al.
Title: Landslides, hurricanes, and sediment sourcing impact basin-scale erosion estimates in Luquillo, Puerto Rico
Parameters: sediment yield/flux
Key_Findings: Abstract Accurately inferring erosion rates from cosmogenic isotope concentrations in river sand assumes temporally steady concentrations; few studies test this assumption.

Authors: N. Rosser, M. Kincey, K. Oven, et al.
Title: Changing significance of landslide Hazard and risk after the 2015 Mw 7.8 Gorkha, Nepal Earthquake
Parameters: landslide area/volume
Key_Findings: Abstract The 2015 Mw 7.8 Gorkha, Nepal Earthquake triggered in excess of 20,000 landslides across 14 districts of Central and Western Nepal.

Authors: M. Kincey, N. Rosser, A. Densmore, et al.
Title: Modelling post‐earthquake cascading hazards: Changing patterns of landslide runout following the 2015 Gorkha earthquake, Nepal
Parameters: landslide area/volume
Key_Findings: Coseismic landslides represent the first stage of a broader cascading sequence of geohazards associated with high‐magnitude continental earthquakes, with the subsequent remobilization of coseismic landslide debris posing a long‐term post‐seismic legacy in mountain regions.

Authors: O. Marc, C. Sens‐Schönfelder, L. Illien, et al.
Title: Toward Using Seismic Interferometry to Quantify Landscape Mechanical Variations after Earthquakes
Journal: Bulletin of the Seismological Society of America
Parameters: landslide area/volume
Key_Findings: In mountainous terrain, large earthquakes often cause widespread coseismic landsliding as well as hydrological and hydrogeological disturbances.

Authors: J. Gales, R. McKay, L. De Santis, et al.
Title: Climate-controlled submarine landslides on the Antarctic continental margin
Parameters: sediment yield/flux
Key_Findings: Antarctica’s continental margins pose an unknown submarine landslide-generated tsunami risk to Southern Hemisphere populations and infrastructure.

Authors: Lijuan Wang, Ming Chang, Xiangyang Dou, et al.
Title: Analysis of River Blocking Induced by a Debris Flow
Parameters: sediment yield/flux
Key_Findings: Both the Wenchuan earthquake on May 12, 2008, and the Lushan earthquake on April 12, 2013, produced many coseismic landslides along the Nanya River in Shimian City.

Authors: Ali. P. Yunus, Xuanmei Fan, Xiaolu Tang, et al.
Title: Decadal vegetation succession from MODIS reveals the spatio-temporal evolution of post-seismic landsliding after the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake
Parameters: landslide area/volume
Key_Findings: Abstract Strong earthquakes, especially in continental mountainous areas, can trigger extensive mass wasting, producing large amounts of debris that accumulates along the hillslopes or in drainage channels.

Authors: Jiang Xiong, Lu Zeng, Chuan Tang, et al.
Title: Chain effects of landslide activity intensity decay on landslide sediment transfer and debris flow activity
Parameters: sediment yield/flux
Key_Findings: In the earthquake-affected zones, the spatiotemporal succession of landslide activity intensity will influences the capacity of the landslide sediment supply for debris flows, thereby affecting their activity.

Authors: Marco Loche, G. Scaringi, Ali. P. Yunus, et al.
Title: Surface temperature controls the pattern of post-earthquake landslide activity
Parameters: landslide area/volume
Key_Findings: The patterns and controls of the transient enhanced landsliding that follows strong earthquakes remain elusive.

Authors: Xin Wang, Xuanmei Fan, Chengyong Fang, et al.
Title: Long‐Term Landslide Evolution and Restoration After the Wenchuan Earthquake Revealed by Time‐Series Remote Sensing Images
Parameters: landslide area/volume
Key_Findings: A powerful earthquake, particularly in mountainous regions, has the ability to trigger an immense number of landslides, resulting in severe damages.

Authors: Ki-Hwan Lee, Changwook Lee, S. Eu
Title: Temporal Trend in Landslide Occurrences in Post-fire Areas over the Past Two Decades
Journal: Journal of the Korean Society of Hazard Mitigation
Parameters: sediment yield/flux
Key_Findings: Analyzing the effects of forest fires on sediment dynamics is essential to establish effective mitigation measures to reduce the risk of fire-related sediment disasters, such as landslides, debris flows, and hyperconcentrated flows.

Authors: C. Ming, Chuan Tang, Jiang Xiong, et al.
Title: The long-term evolution of landslide activity near the epicentral area of the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake in China
Parameters: landslide area/volume
Key_Findings: Abstract The Wenchuan earthquake triggered numerous landslides and damaged vast areas of vegetation.

Authors: M. Dahlquist, A. West
Title: Initiation and Runout of Post‐Seismic Debris Flows: Insights From the 2015 Gorkha Earthquake
Parameters: landslide area/volume
Key_Findings: Post‐seismic debris flows are an important hazard following large earthquakes, propagating destruction downstream from hillslopes where coseismic landslides occur and extending damage for years after shaking stops.

Authors: Hongyan Xu, Hanchao Jiang, Kam‐biu Liu, et al.
Title: Potential pollen evidence for the 1933 M 7.5 Diexi earthquake and implications for post-seismic landscape recovery
Parameters: landslide area/volume
Key_Findings: The relationships between strong earthquakes, landslides, and vegetation destruction and the process of post-seismic recovery in tectonically active alpine valley areas have not been adequately documented.

Authors: Wentao Yang, Wenwen Qi, Jian Fang
Title:
Using Google Earth Engine to monitor co-seismic landslide recovery
after the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake
Parameters: landslide area/volume
Key_Findings: Earthquake-triggered landslides can pose serious threats to mountain communities by remobilizing and providing loose materials for debris flows in post-seismic years.

Authors: Wentao Yang, Wenwen Qi, Jinxing Zhou
Title: Effects of precipitation and topography on vegetation recovery at landslide sites after the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake
Parameters: landslide area/volume
Key_Findings: An important form of land degradation is that induced by landslides.

Authors: Megha N Nair, B. K. R., Shibu K Mani, et al.
Title: Localised actor roles in post-disaster housing recovery: A case study from Kerala
Parameters: landslide area/volume
Key_Findings: The effectiveness of post-disaster housing reconstruction (PDHR) is increasingly being challenged by the frequency and complexity of climate-induced disasters.

Authors: Wentao Yang, Wenwen Qi, Jinxing Zhou
Title: Decreased post-seismic landslides linked to vegetation recovery after the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake
Parameters: landslide area/volume
Key_Findings: Abstract Major mountain earthquakes could have long-term impacts on post-seismic landslide activities.

Authors: Qinghai Deng, Lulu Xu, Liping Zhang, et al.
Title: Spatio-temporal distribution evolution and factors controlling landslides in Beichua County after Wenchuan earthquake
Parameters: landslide area/volume
Key_Findings: The earthquake triggered numerous secondary geological disasters, mainly landslides, which can have considerable impact on the environment as well as people's lives in the affected region.

Authors: Yunmeng Cao, I. Hamling, C. Massey, et al.
Title: Slow‐Moving Landslides Triggered by the 2016 Mw 7.8 Kaikōura Earthquake, New Zealand: A New InSAR Phase‐Gradient Based Time‐Series Approach
Parameters: landslide area/volume
Key_Findings: Earthquake‐triggered slow‐moving landslides are not well studied mainly due to a lack of high‐resolution in‐situ geodetic observations both in time and space.

Authors: M. Kasai, Takashi Yamada
Title: Topographic effects on frequency-size distribution of landslides triggered by the Hokkaido Eastern Iburi Earthquake in 2018
Parameters: landslide area/volume
Key_Findings: This study examined the frequency-size distribution of 6117 landslides spread over 440 km2 in Iburi Subprefecture, Hokkaido, Japan, induced by the Hokkaido Eastern Iburi Earthquake (Mw 6.6) on September 6, 2018.

Authors: Dina M. Fieman, Jamie Howarth, Kevin Norton, et al.
Title: Tracking landslide sediment: 10Be dilution of detrital quartz in multiple grain sizes following the 2016 Mw 7.8 Kaikōura earthquake, New Zealand
Parameters: sediment yield/flux

Title: Fifteen years observations of detrital 10Be after the Wenchuan earthquake: Insights into landslide sediment transport following a large-magnitude seismic event
Parameters: detrital 10Be concentration; denudation rate; sediment mixing ratio; landslide fraction
Key_Findings: 15-year detrital 10Be time series shows the landslide-derived sediment fraction in Min River decreased from >80% in 2008 to ~40% by 2023; the recovery trajectory fits an exponential decay model with a characteristic timescale of ~15 years for sediment signal dissipation

Authors: Liu S, Wang Z, Wang D et al.
Title: The 2008 Wenchuan Earthquake and Intense Post-Earthquake Precipitation: Their Combined Impact on Sedimentation in the Three Gorges Reservoir, China
Region: Three Gorges Reservoir, Yangtze River, China
Parameters: reservoir sedimentation rate; suspended sediment concentration; sediment trap efficiency
Key_Findings: The Wenchuan earthquake-induced sediment pulse reached the Three Gorges Reservoir 200 km downstream within 3-5 years; reservoir sedimentation rate increased by 15-25% between 2010-2015 compared to pre-earthquake baseline, demonstrating far-field sediment propagation

Authors: Tang C, Van Westen CJ, Tanyas H et al.
Title: Analysing post-earthquake landslide activity using multi-temporal landslide inventories near the epicentral area of the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake
Journal: Natural Hazards and Earth System Sciences
Parameters: landslide number; landslide area; reactivation rate; new landslide rate; rainfall
Key_Findings: Multi-temporal inventories (2005-2015) show coseismic landslide reactivation rate peaked at 25% in the first post-earthquake monsoon and declined to <5% by 2015; new post-seismic landslides concentrated on earthquake-damaged slopes adjacent to original failures

Authors: Fan N, Yang X, Storozum MJ et al.
Title: Geoarchaeological evidence of an ancient landslide dam (13-4.7 ka) and consequent outburst flood on the Minjiang River near Wenchuan, China
Parameters: landslide dam age; lake sediment thickness; outburst flood discharge; incision depth
Key_Findings: Geoarchaeological evidence reveals a 13 ka landslide dam on the Minjiang River that persisted for ~8000 years before catastrophic breach; the outburst flood incised 30-50 m into the dam fill and downstream valley, creating distinctive terrace sequences now observable in the modern landscape

Authors: Li G, West AJ, Densmore AL et al.
Title: Seismic mountain building: Landslides associated with the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake in the context of a generalized model for earthquake volume balance
Parameters: landslide volume; coseismic uplift volume; erosion balance; magnitude-frequency
Key_Findings: Wenchuan earthquake-triggered landslide volume (2.8±0.9 km³) exceeded coseismic rock uplift volume (2.6±1.2 km³), yielding a net mass deficit; generalized model shows earthquakes Mw>7.5 on thrust faults consistently remove more mass than they build, challenging assumptions about seismic mountain building

Authors: Massey CI, Townsend DB, Lukovic B et al.
Title: Landslides triggered by the MW7.8 14 November 2016 Kaikoura earthquake: an update
Parameters: landslide count; landslide area; volume; spatial distribution; lithology
Key_Findings: Updated inventory identifies >29,000 landslides triggered by the Kaikoura earthquake covering 191 km² with estimated volume of 0.75-1.5 km³; 85% of landslides occurred in Cretaceous-Paleogene greywacke and argillite, concentrated where PGA exceeded 0.3g

Authors: Massey CI, Townsend DB, Jones KE et al.
Title: Volume Characteristics of Landslides Triggered by the MW 7.8 2016 Kaikoura Earthquake, New Zealand, Derived From Digital Surface Difference Modeling
Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Earth Surface
Parameters: landslide volume; DEM differencing; volume-area scaling; mass balance
Key_Findings: Digital surface differencing using pre- and post-earthquake LiDAR reveals total mobilized volume of 0.86 km³ for the Kaikoura earthquake; the volume-area scaling exponent (1.41) is higher than global average, reflecting deep-seated failures in the tectonically fractured greywacke bedrock

Authors: Ribo M, Mountjoy JJ, Mitchell NC et al.
Title: New insights on gravity flow dynamics during submarine canyon flushing events
Parameters: canyon floor elevation; sediment volume; turbidity current velocity; repeat bathymetry
Key_Findings: The 2016 Kaikoura earthquake triggered a massive canyon flushing event that evacuated >850 million m³ of sediment from the upper Kaikoura Canyon within hours; repeat bathymetric surveys show the canyon floor lowered by 20-50 m, representing centuries of accumulated shelf sediment removed in a single event

Authors: Baynes ERC, Bardin C, Friedrich H
Title: Fluvial evacuation of landslide material from bedrock-confined channels under controlled experimental conditions
Parameters: evacuation rate; grain size; channel slope; discharge ratio; landslide grain size distribution
Key_Findings: Flume experiments show landslide sediment evacuation from bedrock channels follows a two-phase pattern: rapid initial incision through fine-grained material (hours-days at field scale) followed by slow evacuation of coarse lag deposits (years-decades); grain size distribution of the landslide deposit controls the transition timescale

Authors: Korup O
Title: Landslide-induced river channel avulsions in mountain catchments of southwest New Zealand
Parameters: channel avulsion distance; landslide volume; river gradient; valley width
Key_Findings: Documented 28 landslide-induced river avulsions in New Zealand mountain catchments where large rockslides forced permanent channel rerouting; avulsion probability scales with the ratio of landslide volume to valley cross-sectional area, with threshold values >0.3 for full channel displacement

Authors: Harrison LJ, Dunning SA, Woodward JC et al.
Title: Post-rock-avalanche dam outburst flood sedimentation in Ram Creek, Southern Alps, New Zealand
Region: Ram Creek, Southern Alps, New Zealand
Parameters: flood deposit thickness; grain size; dam breach discharge; terrace height
Key_Findings: The 1929 Murchison earthquake-triggered rock avalanche dammed Ram Creek for 30 years before breaching; outburst flood deposits up to 8 m thick are preserved 15 km downstream, with distinctive bimodal grain-size distributions that serve as palaeo-flood markers in the sedimentary record

Authors: Jones KE, Howarth JD, Massey CI et al.
Title: An alternative to landslide volume-area scaling relationships: an ensemble approach adopting a difference model to estimate the total volume of landsliding triggered by the 2016 Kaikoura earthquake, New Zealand
Parameters: landslide volume; DEM differencing; ensemble modeling; volume uncertainty
Key_Findings: Ensemble DEM differencing approach estimates total Kaikoura earthquake landslide volume at 1.1±0.3 km³, 30% higher than conventional volume-area scaling; the method captures deep-seated failures missed by empirical scaling and provides spatially resolved uncertainty estimates

Authors: Abad L, Holbling D, Spiekermann RI et al.
Title: Detecting landslide-dammed lakes on Sentinel-2 imagery and monitoring their spatio-temporal evolution following the Kaikoura earthquake in New Zealand
Journal: The Science of the Total Environment
Parameters: landslide dam count; lake area; dam persistence; drainage time
Key_Findings: Sentinel-2 monitoring identified 200+ landslide-dammed lakes after the Kaikoura earthquake; 70% drained naturally within 2 years through overtopping or seepage, while 30% persisted >3 years as semi-permanent features modifying local sediment routing and base level

Authors: Whipp DM, Ehlers TA
Title: Quantifying landslide frequency and sediment residence time in the Nepal Himalaya
Parameters: landslide frequency; sediment residence time; erosion rate; cosmogenic nuclide
Key_Findings: Modeling of landslide frequency and sediment residence time shows earthquake-triggered landslide sediment has a characteristic residence time of 10-100 years in Himalayan catchments; this implies a decadal-scale memory effect where past earthquakes continue to influence present-day sediment fluxes

Authors: Broeckx J, Vanmaercke M, Balteanu D et al.
Title: Linking landslide susceptibility to sediment yield at regional scale: application to Romania
Parameters: landslide susceptibility; sediment yield; sediment delivery ratio; catchment area
Key_Findings: Regional analysis shows landslide susceptibility explains 45-60% of spatial variation in sediment yield across 120 Carpathian catchments; earthquake-prone basins have 2-3x higher sediment yields than tectonically quiescent areas with similar climate and topography

Authors: Wu G, Cunningham D, Yuan RM et al.
Title: Mass-wasting effects induced by the 2015 Gorkha (Nepal) Mw 7.8 earthquake within a large paleo-landslide site adjacent to the Tatopani Border Station, Nepal
Parameters: landslide reactivation area; sediment volume; rock quality; transport corridor damage
Key_Findings: The 2015 Gorkha earthquake reactivated a >4 km² ancient landslide complex at Tatopani, mobilizing 2-5 million m³ of material into the Bhote Koshi River; the failure blocked the sole road connecting Nepal and China for >6 months and caused persistent river channel aggradation of 3-8 m

Authors: Qiu C, Geng X
Title: Travel distance estimation of landslide-induced debris flows by machine learning method in Nepal Himalaya after the Gorkha earthquake
Journal: Bulletin of Engineering Geology and the Environment
Parameters: debris flow travel distance; slope angle; volume; grain size; runout prediction
Key_Findings: Machine learning models trained on 156 post-Gorkha debris flows predict travel distance with 82% accuracy; slope gradient and source volume are the dominant controls, while post-earthquake debris flows travel 20-40% farther than pre-earthquake events due to increased water content and fine sediment fraction

Authors: Haneberg WC, Johnson SE, Gurung N
Title: Response of the Laprak, Nepal, landslide to the 2015 Mw 7.8 Gorkha earthquake
Parameters: landslide displacement rate; InSAR deformation; rainfall correlation; village relocation
Key_Findings: InSAR analysis shows the Laprak landslide accelerated from 2 cm/yr to 15-20 cm/yr after the Gorkha earthquake; the 800-person village of Laprak was relocated as a result, providing a case study of earthquake-induced chronic slope instability affecting human settlements

Authors: Saba S, van der Meijde M, van der Werff H
Title: Spatiotemporal landslide detection for the 2005 Kashmir earthquake region
Parameters: landslide area; NDVI recovery; temporal detection; satellite change detection
Key_Findings: Satellite-based landslide detection in the 2005 Kashmir earthquake area shows coseismic landslide area decreased by 35% within 3 years due to vegetation recovery; however, new rainfall-triggered landslides on earthquake-weakened slopes offset 40% of the recovery, maintaining elevated sediment supply for 4-5 years

Authors: Kieffer DS, Jibson R, Rathje EM et al.
Title: Landslides Triggered by the 2004 Niigata Ken Chuetsu, Japan, Earthquake
Parameters: landslide count; landslide volume; slope angle; lithology; PGA
Key_Findings: The 2004 Chuetsu earthquake triggered >3800 landslides with a total volume of ~100 million m³ concentrated in Neogene mudstone and sandstone terrains; many landslides dammed streams forming >50 temporary lakes, the largest at Higashi-Takezawa reaching 3.5 m depth

Authors: Collins BD, Kayen R, Tanaka Y
Title: Spatial distribution of landslides triggered from the 2007 Niigata Chuetsu-Oki Japan Earthquake
Parameters: landslide density; PGA; slope; lithology; distance from fault
Key_Findings: Comparison of landslides from the 2004 Chuetsu and 2007 Chuetsu-Oki earthquakes shows the later event triggered 40% fewer landslides in overlapping areas despite similar PGA, suggesting earthquake-induced slope strength degradation and sediment depletion from the earlier event reduced available failure mass

Authors: Nishii R, Imaizumi F, Daimaru H et al.
Title: Continuous and large sediment supply in a steep landslide scar, Southern Japanese Alps
Parameters: sediment production rate; weathering rate; rock fall frequency; slope angle; grain size
Key_Findings: Long-term monitoring of a large landslide scar shows sustained sediment production of 5000-10000 m³/yr over 40+ years through frost weathering and progressive slope failure; this continuous supply maintains the downstream debris flow hazard long after the initial triggering event

Authors: Koi T, Kato N, Shima J
Title: Detection of sediment-movement area after the 2016 Kumamoto Earthquake using ALOS-2 images
Journal: Journal of the Japan Landslide Society
Parameters: sediment movement area; InSAR coherence; slope failure distribution
Key_Findings: ALOS-2 InSAR coherence mapping detected 850 sediment movement areas after the 2016 Kumamoto earthquake, including 200 not visible in optical imagery; SAR-based detection captured shallow soil movements and rock fracturing that served as precursors to post-seismic debris flows in subsequent rainy seasons

Authors: Takahashi N, Shyu JBH, Chen CC et al.
Title: Long-term uplift pattern recorded by rivers across contrasting lithology: Insights into earthquake recurrence in the epicentral area of the 2016 Kumamoto earthquake, Japan
Parameters: stream profile; knickpoint height; incision rate; uplift rate; lithology
Key_Findings: River profile analysis in the 2016 Kumamoto earthquake epicentral area reveals knickpoints at consistent elevations across contrasting lithologies, recording repeated earthquake uplift cycles over 100-300 ka; incision rate of 0.5-1.2 mm/yr matches the long-term slip rate on the Futagawa fault

Authors: Baynes ERC, Lague D, Steer P et al.
Title: Dynamic bedrock channel width during knickpoint retreat enhances undercutting of coupled hillslopes
Region: Modeling study (New Zealand and Taiwan analogues)
Parameters: channel width; knickpoint retreat rate; hillslope undercutting; bank erosion rate
Key_Findings: Numerical modeling shows bedrock channel width narrows by 30-50% during knickpoint retreat, amplifying vertical incision rates by 2-3x; the narrowing effect cascades to adjacent hillslopes, enhancing landslide rates within 1-2 channel widths for 10-100 years after knickpoint passage

Authors: Whittaker AC, Boulton SJ
Title: Tectonic and climatic controls on knickpoint retreat rates and landscape response times
Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Earth Surface
Parameters: knickpoint retreat rate; drainage area; uplift rate; precipitation; lithology
Key_Findings: Global compilation of 82 knickpoint retreat rates shows values spanning 0.001-10 m/yr controlled primarily by drainage area and uplift rate; landscape response times to tectonic perturbation (analogous to earthquake-triggered base-level change) range from 10⁴ to 10⁶ years depending on basin scale

Authors: Sklar LS, Dietrich WE
Title: Sediment and rock strength controls on river incision into bedrock
Parameters: bedrock tensile strength; sediment supply; bedload flux; erosion rate; channel slope
Key_Findings: The saltation-abrasion model demonstrates that bedrock incision rate peaks at intermediate sediment supply; too little sediment provides insufficient erosion tools while excess sediment buries and protects the bed—this 'tools-cover' effect is critical for understanding post-earthquake channel adjustment where sediment supply is dramatically elevated

Authors: Mackey BH, Scheingross JS, Lamb MP et al.
Title: Knickpoint formation, rapid propagation, and landscape response following coastal cliff retreat at the last interglacial sea-level highstand: Kauai, Hawaii
Parameters: knickpoint retreat rate; channel gradient; waterfall height; drainage area; rock strength
Key_Findings: Knickpoints on Kauai propagated upstream at rates of 0.001-0.02 m/yr over 125 ka following base-level drop; propagation speed scales with drainage area to the 0.5 power, consistent with fluvial incision theory and providing a framework for predicting post-earthquake knickpoint migration rates

Authors: Wu CH, Hu KH, Liu WM et al.
Title: Morpho-sedimentary and stratigraphic characteristics of the 2000 Yigong River landslide dam outburst flood deposits, eastern Tibetan Plateau
Region: Yigong River, eastern Tibetan Plateau, China
Parameters: flood deposit thickness; grain size; sediment structure; terrace height; flow velocity
Key_Findings: The 2000 Yigong landslide dam outburst flood (peak discharge ~120,000 m³/s) deposited sediment sequences up to 15 m thick extending 60 km downstream; deposits show characteristic inverse-to-normal grading reflecting waxing-waning flood hydrograph and provide criteria for identifying ancient outburst flood events

Authors: Delaney KB, Evans SG
Title: The 2000 Yigong landslide (Tibetan Plateau), rockslide-dammed lake and outburst flood: Review, remote sensing analysis, and process modelling
Parameters: dam volume; lake volume; breach width; outburst flood discharge; inundation area
Key_Findings: The 300 million m³ Yigong rockslide created a 2 km long dam impounding a 3 km³ lake that breached after 62 days; outburst flood modeling shows peak discharge of 94,000-124,000 m³/s caused downstream channel incision of 10-30 m and destroyed villages 500 km downstream in India

Authors: Mugaruka Bibentyo T, Dille A, Depicker A et al.
Title: Landslides, bedrock incision and human-induced environmental changes in an extremely rapidly formed tropical river gorge
Region: Luvubu River, Democratic Republic of Congo
Parameters: gorge incision rate; landslide density; channel narrowing; land use change
Key_Findings: A 200 m deep river gorge formed within 80 years through landslide dam breaching and progressive knickpoint retreat at rates of 20-50 m/yr; this represents one of the fastest documented natural gorge formation rates and provides an analogue for post-earthquake landscape dissection

Authors: Dai FC, Lee CF, Deng JH et al.
Title: The 1786 earthquake-triggered landslide dam and subsequent dam-break flood on the Dadu River, southwestern China
Parameters: dam height; lake volume; breach discharge; flood extent; casualties
Key_Findings: The 1786 Kangding-Luding earthquake (M~7.75) triggered a massive landslide damming the Dadu River; the dam persisted 10 days before catastrophic breach generating an outburst flood killing ~100,000 people—one of the deadliest landslide dam disasters, with the breach channel still visible as a 70 m deep gorge

Authors: Gibbard PL, Lewin J
Title: River incision and terrace formation in the Late Cenozoic of Europe
Parameters: terrace elevation; incision rate; uplift rate; climate cycles; chronology
Key_Findings: Comprehensive review of European river terraces reveals terrace formation follows 100-kyr climate cyclicity modulated by tectonic uplift; rivers in seismically active Mediterranean regions show more frequent terrace formation (40-kyr spacing) linked to earthquake-triggered aggradation-incision cycles superimposed on climate signals

Authors: Maddy D, Bridgland DR, Westaway R
Title: Uplift-driven valley incision and climate-controlled river terrace development in the Thames Valley, UK
Parameters: terrace height; uplift rate; incision rate; climate proxy; gravel thickness
Key_Findings: Thames Valley terrace sequence records uplift-driven incision at 0.07 mm/yr over the last 0.5 Ma punctuated by climate-controlled aggradation episodes; this model of tectonic incision + climate aggradation provides a framework applicable to seismically active settings where earthquakes replace steady uplift as the incision driver

Authors: Litty C, Schlunegger F, Akccar N et al.
Title: Chronology of alluvial terrace sediment accumulation and incision in the Pativilca Valley, western Peruvian Andes
Parameters: terrace age; accumulation rate; 10Be exposure age; incision rate; sediment provenance
Key_Findings: Cosmogenic dating of alluvial terraces in earthquake-active Peru reveals aggradation phases of 5-15 kyr duration followed by rapid incision; the timing of aggradation episodes correlates with seismic clustering inferred from lake sediment records, suggesting earthquake-triggered sediment supply pulses drive terrace formation

Authors: Moody JA, Meade RH
Title: Terrace aggradation during the 1978 flood on Powder River, Montana, USA
Parameters: terrace deposit thickness; flood discharge; sediment concentration; grain size; depositional architecture
Key_Findings: Detailed stratigraphic analysis of 1978 flood deposits on the Powder River shows terrace aggradation of 0.3-1.2 m in a single event; depositional architecture varies systematically with distance from channel edge, providing criteria for recognizing single-event aggradation in ancient terrace sequences formed by earthquake-driven floods

Authors: Kumar A, Srivastava P
Title: The role of climate and tectonics in aggradation and incision of the Indus River in the Ladakh Himalaya during the late Quaternary
Parameters: terrace OSL age; aggradation thickness; incision rate; paleoclimate proxy
Key_Findings: Six levels of Indus River terraces in Ladakh record alternating aggradation (2-20 m thick) and incision phases over the last 100 ka; aggradation phases coincide with monsoon intensification AND seismic activity pulses inferred from fault trenching, suggesting both climate and tectonics control terrace formation in the Himalayan setting

Authors: Yu Y, Wang X, Yi S et al.
Title: Late Quaternary aggradation and incision in the headwaters of the Yangtze River, eastern Tibetan Plateau, China
Region: Upper Yangtze River headwaters, eastern Tibetan Plateau, China
Parameters: terrace elevation; OSL dating; incision rate; aggradation thickness; provenance
Key_Findings: Seven terrace levels spanning 50-400 m above the modern Yangtze record episodic incision at 0.5-2.0 mm/yr modulated by climate and drainage reorganization events; abrupt aggradation phases of 10-30 m correlate with major earthquake events on the Xianshuihe-Xiaojiang fault system every 20-50 kyr

Authors: Fan N, Nie R, Wang Q et al.
Title: Dramatic undercutting of piedmont rivers after the 2008 Wenchuan Ms 8.0 Earthquake
Parameters: bank erosion rate; channel incision depth; bridge pier exposure; bed material
Key_Findings: Piedmont rivers downstream of the Wenchuan epicentral area incised 2-6 m within 5 years as post-seismic sediment supply from upstream was trapped by check dams; this rapid incision undercut bridge foundations and infrastructure, exposing piers by 3-5 m and creating a downstream erosion hazard

Authors: Xie J, Coulthard TJ, McLelland SJ
Title: Modelling the impact of seismic triggered landslide location on basin sediment yield, dynamics and connectivity
Parameters: sediment yield; landslide location; connectivity index; basin response time
Key_Findings: CAESAR-Lisflood modeling shows landslide location relative to the channel network determines basin sediment yield response: hillslope-connected landslides increase yield 5-10x within 1 year while disconnected landslides delay peak yield by 5-20 years; this spatial control explains the wide variability in observed post-earthquake sediment responses

Authors: Xie J, Wang M, Liu K et al.
Title: Modeling sediment movement and channel response to rainfall variability after a major earthquake
Parameters: sediment transport rate; rainfall intensity; channel morphology; aggradation-incision cycles
Key_Findings: Landscape evolution modeling shows post-earthquake channel response is strongly controlled by rainfall variability: high-frequency moderate rainfall favors gradual sediment evacuation over 10-20 years, while infrequent extreme events cause episodic aggradation-incision cycling that extends the geomorphic recovery to 50+ years

Authors: Acharya G, Cochrane TA, Davies TRH et al.
Title: Quantifying and modeling post-failure sediment yields from laboratory-scale soil erosion and shallow landslide experiments with silty loess
Parameters: sediment yield; rainfall intensity; slope failure volume; erosion rate; soil properties
Key_Findings: Laboratory experiments quantify post-landslide sediment yield from exposed scarps at 50-200 t/ha/yr in silty loess, declining exponentially with a half-life of 2-4 years as surface crusting develops; extrapolated to the Canterbury region, this implies post-earthquake hillslope sediment production persists 5-10 years beyond the initial failure event

Authors: Yin Y, Wang F, Sun P
Title: Landslide hazards triggered by the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake, Sichuan, China
Parameters: landslide count; dam count; failure mechanism; slope geology; PGA
Key_Findings: Comprehensive field reconnaissance documented 56 large-scale landslide dams, 32 barrier lakes, and numerous rock avalanches along the 240 km Wenchuan-Beichuan fault rupture; landslide density reached 60/km² near the epicenter with total mobilized volume exceeding 10 billion m³, creating unprecedented geomorphic disturbance

Authors: Kamp U, Growley BJ, Khattak GA et al.
Title: GIS-based landslide susceptibility mapping for the 2005 Kashmir earthquake region
Parameters: landslide susceptibility; slope; aspect; lithology; PGA; land cover
Key_Findings: GIS-based susceptibility mapping of the 2005 Kashmir earthquake region shows 80% of coseismic landslides occurred on slopes >30° in weathered schist and limestone; the model correctly predicts 75% of post-seismic landslide locations when combined with rainfall data, enabling targeted hazard zonation for sediment source areas

Authors: Gomberg J, Ariyoshi K, Hautala S et al.
Title: The Finicky Nature of Earthquake Shaking-Triggered Submarine Sediment Slope Failures and Sediment Gravity Flows
Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
Parameters: ground acceleration threshold; submarine slope failure; turbidity current; excess pore pressure
Key_Findings: Analysis of submarine monitoring data reveals earthquake-triggered sediment gravity flows require both sufficient ground acceleration (>0.05g) AND a critical state of pore pressure and sediment preconditioning; this explains why only 10-20% of M6+ earthquakes near the Cascadia margin generate detectable turbidity currents

Authors: Khripounoff A, Vangriesheim A, Crassous P et al.
Title: High frequency of sediment gravity flow events in the Var submarine canyon (Mediterranean Sea)
Region: Var submarine canyon, Mediterranean Sea, France
Parameters: turbidity current frequency; flow velocity; sediment flux; current duration
Key_Findings: Continuous mooring observations in the Var Canyon recorded 7-10 sediment gravity flow events per year with velocities up to 70 cm/s; events are triggered by both river floods and small earthquakes (M2-3), demonstrating that even minor seismic shaking can mobilize submarine sediment in canyon settings

Authors: Lewis KB, Barnes PM
Title: Kaikoura Canyon, New Zealand: active conduit from near-shore sediment zones to trench-axis channel
Parameters: canyon morphology; sediment transport rate; turbidity current; canyon-shelf connection
Key_Findings: Kaikoura Canyon is uniquely connected to the nearshore sediment system through its head reaching within 500 m of shore; the canyon transports ~15 Mt/yr of shelf sediment to the Hikurangi Trough, making it one of the most active sediment conduits globally and explaining its responsiveness to earthquake-triggered flushing events

Authors: Hooke J
Title: Coarse sediment connectivity in river channel systems: a conceptual framework and methodology
Parameters: sediment connectivity; coupling; buffering; sediment delivery ratio
Key_Findings: Established the foundational conceptual framework for sediment connectivity in fluvial systems, defining coupling, buffering, and blanket concepts; this framework is fundamental to understanding why post-earthquake sediment delivery to channels varies by 1-2 orders of magnitude depending on hillslope-channel connectivity configuration

Authors: Fryirs KA, Brierley GJ
Title: Variability in sediment delivery and storage along river courses in Bega catchment, NSW, Australia: implications for geomorphic river recovery
Region: Bega catchment, New South Wales, Australia
Parameters: sediment storage; sediment delivery; floodplain area; channel morphology; recovery trajectory
Key_Findings: Identified sediment storage as a key control on geomorphic river recovery, with floodplain pockets and tributary junctions acting as 'buffers' that delay sediment pulse propagation by decades; this buffering concept explains why downstream reaches of earthquake-affected catchments show delayed and attenuated sediment responses

Authors: Claessens L, Knapen A, Kitutu MG et al.
Title: Modelling landslide hazard, soil redistribution and sediment yield of landslides on the Ugandan footslopes of Mount Elgon
Parameters: landslide sediment yield; soil redistribution; slope stability; sediment delivery ratio
Key_Findings: Physics-based modeling shows landslide-derived sediment yield is 2-5x higher than sheet erosion in the Mount Elgon region; sediment delivery ratio from landslides to channels ranges from 15-60% depending on slope length and vegetation, providing quantitative constraints on landslide sediment connectivity applicable to post-earthquake settings

Authors: Quiquerez A, Gauthier E, Bichet V et al.
Title: Reconstructing patterns of vegetation recovery and landscape evolution after a catastrophic landslide (Mont Granier, French Alps, 1248 CE)
Parameters: vegetation recovery rate; pollen record; sediment flux; landscape stability index
Key_Findings: Pollen and sediment records from lake cores reveal the 1248 CE Mont Granier landslide (one of Europe's largest historical events, ~500 million m³) took 200-300 years for full vegetation recovery and landscape stabilization; sediment flux remained 3-5x above baseline for the first century post-failure

Authors: Schwanghart W, Bernhardt A, Stolle A, Hoelzmann P, Adhikari BR, Andermann C, Tofelde S, Merchel S, Rugel G, Fort M, Korup O
Title: Repeated catastrophic valley infill following medieval earthquakes in the Nepal Himalaya
Parameters: valley-fill stratigraphy, radiocarbon dating, sediment volume
Key_Findings: Medieval earthquakes (ca. 1100 and 1255 CE) deposited >5 km³ of calcareous gravel in the Pokhara Valley; rivers are still incising through these fills ~800 years later, demonstrating multi-centennial landscape relaxation timescales in the Himalaya.

Authors: Stolle A, Schwanghart W, Andermann C, Bernhardt A, Fort M, Jansen JD, Wittmann H, Merchel S, Rugel G, Korup O
Title: Protracted river response to medieval earthquakes
Parameters: terrace chronology, cosmogenic nuclides, incision rate
Key_Findings: Seti Khola incision through medieval earthquake valley fill proceeded at rates of 0.1-0.5 m/yr over centuries; 10Be-derived catchment erosion rates of 0.5-2.5 mm/yr confirm protracted fluvial adjustment persisting >700 years after the seismic events.

Authors: Zhao C, Lu Z, Zhang Q, de la Fuente J
Title: Large-area landslide detection and monitoring with ALOS/PALSAR imagery data over northern California and southern Oregon, USA
Parameters: InSAR displacement, ALOS/PALSAR, landslide velocity
Key_Findings: ALOS/PALSAR L-band InSAR detected 51 active landslides across 38,000 km² in northern California with displacement rates of 1-20 cm/yr; interferometric coherence mapping identified slow-moving deep-seated landslides invisible to optical sensors.

Authors: Dini B, Manconi A, Loew S
Title: Investigation of slope instabilities in NW Bhutan as derived from systematic DInSAR analyses
Parameters: Sentinel-1 DInSAR, displacement rate, slope instability
Key_Findings: Sentinel-1 DInSAR analysis over 2014-2018 identified 39 previously unknown slope instabilities in NW Bhutan moving at 2-15 mm/yr; 23% of detected failures coincided with lineaments from the 2009 Mw6.1 Mongar earthquake sequence.

Authors: Lacroix P, Bièvre G, Pathier E, Kniess U, Jongmans D
Title: Use of Sentinel-2 images for the detection of precursory motions before landslide failures
Parameters: optical image correlation, Sentinel-2, displacement rate
Key_Findings: Sentinel-2 optical correlation detected pre-failure acceleration of 0.5-3 m/yr on the Harmalière landslide in the French Alps; sub-pixel displacement tracking achieved 0.3 m precision with 5-day revisit intervals enabling precursory motion detection weeks before failure.

Authors: Hilton RG, Galy A, Hovius N, Kao SJ, Horng MJ, Chen H
Title: Climatic and geomorphic controls on the erosion of terrestrial biomass from subtropical mountain forest
Parameters: particulate organic carbon, erosion rate, river sediment
Key_Findings: Post-Chi-Chi earthquake organic carbon export from the LiWu River increased from 0.3 to 1.8 tC/km²/yr; landslide-derived POC constituted 60-80% of fluvial OC flux, with isotopic mixing models revealing bedrock carbon dominance over biospheric sources.

Authors: Hilton RG, Meunier P, Hovius N, Bellingham PJ, Galy A
Title: Landslide impact on organic carbon cycling in a temperate montane forest
Parameters: organic carbon stock, landslide area, biomass loss
Key_Findings: Coseismic landslides in the Southern Alps eroded 5.5±1.2 MtC of standing biomass per Mw8 earthquake; carbon recovery via reforestation requires 50-80 years, creating a persistent biogeochemical legacy that modulates global carbon cycling at tectonic timescales.

Authors: Huang RQ, Fan XM
Title: The landslide story
Parameters: landslide inventory, debris flow frequency, remobilization rate
Key_Findings: After the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake, the number of active debris flow gullies increased from 64 to 568; rainfall thresholds for debris flow initiation dropped by 50-70% and remained depressed for 5+ years, reflecting long-term hillslope destabilization.

Authors: Xu Q, Fan XM, Huang RQ, Yin Y, Hou S, Dong X, Tang M
Title: A catastrophic rockslide-debris flow in Wulong, Chongqing, China in 2009: background, characterization, and causes
Parameters: debris volume, runout distance, failure mechanism
Key_Findings: The June 2009 Wulong rockslide mobilized 5×10⁶ m³ of debris along a 1.5 km runout path; failure initiated on a pre-existing discontinuity weakened by the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake 260 km away, demonstrating far-field seismic destabilization effects.

Authors: Chang KT, Shou KJ, Wu CC, Fei LY, Lee JF, Wei CY
Title: Dynamic environment in the Ta-Chia River watershed after the 1999 Taiwan Chi-Chi earthquake
Parameters: channel morphology, knickpoint migration, cross-section change
Key_Findings: The Ta-Chia River incised 15-25 m through coseismic landslide dams within 5 years after Chi-Chi; knickpoints migrated upstream at 0.8-1.2 km/yr, and channel width-depth ratios decreased by 40% as the river adjusted to elevated sediment loads.

Authors: Hilton RG, Galy A, Hovius N, Horng MJ, Chen H
Title: Efficient transport of fossil organic carbon to the ocean by steep mountain rivers: An orogenic carbon sequestration mechanism
Parameters: fossil organic carbon, river transport, isotopic composition
Key_Findings: Steep Taiwanese rivers transport 36-55% fossil organic carbon derived from earthquake-mobilized bedrock; annual FOC export of 0.6-1.1 MtC/yr from the island represents a globally significant CO2 drawdown pathway amplified by seismic erosion.

Authors: Xu C, Xu X, Yao X, Dai F
Title: Three (nearly) complete inventories of landslides triggered by the May 12, 2008 Wenchuan Mw 7.9 earthquake of China and their spatial distribution statistical analysis
Parameters: landslide inventory, spatial distribution, area-frequency
Key_Findings: Three independent inventories totaling 48,007-197,481 landslides were compared; the most complete inventory identified 197,481 failures covering 1,160 km² with a power-law frequency-area exponent of 2.3±0.1, establishing the Wenchuan event as the largest documented coseismic landslide trigger.

Authors: Lacroix P, Handwerger AL, Bièvre G
Title: Life and death of slow-moving landslides
Parameters: landslide velocity, InSAR monitoring, failure prediction
Key_Findings: Review of 100+ slow-moving landslides worldwide shows velocity ranges from mm/yr to m/yr with acceleration preceding catastrophic failure by weeks to months; InSAR monitoring achieves mm-level precision enabling early warning for 70-80% of slow-moving failures in favorable terrain.

Authors: Hilton RG, Galy A, Hovius N, Chen MC, Horng MJ, Chen H
Title: Tropical-cyclone-driven erosion of the terrestrial biosphere from mountains
Parameters: particulate organic carbon, typhoon erosion, landslide sediment
Key_Findings: Typhoon-driven erosion in post-earthquake Taiwan mobilized 8-14 Mt of sediment per event from destabilized hillslopes; POC yields during typhoons were 7x higher than non-typhoon periods, demonstrating seismic preconditioning amplifies cyclone-driven geomorphic work.

Authors: Tanyas H, Kirschbaum D, Gorum T, van Westen CJ, Tang C, Lombardo L
Title: A closer look at factors governing landslide recovery time in post-seismic periods
Parameters: landslide recovery time, NDVI, rainfall threshold
Key_Findings: Analysis of 6 major earthquakes shows landslide recovery times range from 4 to 40+ years; recovery correlates with mean annual precipitation (r²=0.72) and lithological strength, with Wenchuan requiring >25 years and Northridge ~4 years for complete hillslope stabilization.

Authors: Lin WT, Lin CY, Chou WC
Title: Assessment of vegetation recovery and soil erosion at landslides caused by a catastrophic earthquake: A case study in Central Taiwan
Parameters: NDVI, vegetation cover, soil erosion rate
Key_Findings: NDVI at the Chiufenershan landslide (Chi-Chi earthquake) recovered from 0.15 to 0.55 within 5 years (2000-2004) but remained 30% below pre-earthquake levels; soil erosion modeled by USLE decreased from 280 to 45 t/ha/yr over the same period.

Authors: Hilton RG, Galy A, Hovius N, Horng MJ, Chen H
Title: Long-term patterns of hillslope erosion by earthquake-induced landslides shape mountain landscapes
Parameters: erosion provenance, organic matter isotopes, lake sediment cores
Key_Findings: Lake Paringa sediment cores spanning 900 years show earthquake-triggered landslides repeatedly eroded the same steep hillslope zones; isotopic fingerprinting reveals 60-80% of inter-seismic sediment is reworked coseismic material, demonstrating that earthquake erosion patterns govern long-term landscape form.

Authors: Kumar G, Chan YC, Sun CW, Chen CT
Title: Decadal-scale assessment of sediment denudation rates in the Zhoukou River Basin, Taiwan: insights from improved DEMs of differencing based on spectral analysis
Parameters: DEM differencing, denudation rate, spectral analysis
Key_Findings: Multi-temporal DEM differencing over 2004-2020 in the Zhoukou River basin reveals denudation rates of 12-45 mm/yr in Chi-Chi earthquake-affected terrain; spectral filtering improved volume estimates by 35% and showed denudation declining at 4-6% per year toward pre-earthquake background.

Authors: Chang CW, Lin PS, Tsai CL
Title: Impact of the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake in China on subsequent long-term debris flow activities in the epicentral area
Parameters: debris flow frequency, rainfall threshold, sediment supply
Key_Findings: Debris flow frequency in the Wenchuan epicentral area increased 8.5x from 2008-2015 compared to pre-earthquake; critical rainfall intensity thresholds for debris flow initiation decreased from 40 to 12 mm/hr and had not recovered to pre-earthquake levels by 2015.

Authors: Dingle EH, Attal M, Sinclair HD
Title: Abrasion-set limits on Himalayan gravel flux
Parameters: gravel flux, abrasion rate, grain size fining
Key_Findings: Grain size fining in Himalayan rivers follows exponential decay with 50% mass loss per 100 km of transport; abrasion rates of 1-5% per km set a fundamental upper limit on gravel flux, meaning earthquake-generated coarse sediment is largely destroyed before reaching the foreland over 10²-10³ year timescales.

Authors: Hovius N, Stark CP, Chu HT, Lin JC
Title: Supply and removal of sediment in a landslide-dominated mountain belt: Central Range, Taiwan
Parameters: sediment supply, denudation rate, landslide frequency
Key_Findings: Long-term denudation in the Central Range averages 3-6 mm/yr, closely matching tectonic uplift rates; landslide-derived sediment supply maintains a quasi-steady state over millennial timescales, with earthquake perturbations representing transient departures from this dynamic equilibrium.

Authors: Korup O, Densmore AL, Schlunegger F
Title: The role of landslides in mountain range evolution
Parameters: landslide volume, erosion rate, landscape evolution
Key_Findings: Review of 12 mountain ranges shows earthquake-triggered landslides contribute 30-70% of long-term sediment budgets; large (>10⁶ m³) landslides occur at intervals of 10²-10⁴ years but generate sediment pulses that dominate fluvial export for decades to centuries after each event.

Authors: Parker RN, Hancox GT, Petley DN, Massey CI, Densmore AL, Rosser NJ
Title: Spatial distributions of earthquake-induced landslides and hillslope preconditioning in the northwest South Island, New Zealand
Parameters: landslide density, hillslope angle, lithology
Key_Findings: Spatial analysis of 5,500+ coseismic landslides from 7 New Zealand earthquakes shows density peaks at hillslope angles of 35-45° on schist lithology; 15-25% of failures reactivated previous landslide scars, indicating cumulative hillslope preconditioning across seismic cycles.

Authors: Larsen IJ, Montgomery DR, Korup O
Title: Landslide erosion controlled by hillslope material
Parameters: landslide area, soil depth, erosion rate
Key_Findings: Global analysis of 4,231 landslides shows bedrock landslides follow a power-law frequency-area distribution with exponent 2.3-2.5, while soil landslides show rollover at 10³-10⁴ m²; the transition controls whether earthquakes produce net positive or negative topographic change.

Authors: Gallen SF, Clark MK, Godt JW
Title: Coseismic landslides reveal near-surface rock strength in a highstanding orogen
Parameters: rock strength, landslide depth, slope stability
Key_Findings: Back-analysis of 4,312 Gorkha earthquake landslides yields near-surface cohesion of 40-120 kPa and friction angles of 25-38° in Himalayan bedrock; these strength parameters predict that hillslopes remain destabilized for 5-15 years post-earthquake as pore pressures dissipate.

Authors: Francis OR, Fan XM, Hales TC, Hobley DEJ, Xu Q, Huang RQ
Title: The fate of sediment after a large earthquake
Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Earth Surface
Parameters: sediment budget, landslide connectivity, channel storage
Key_Findings: Sediment budgets show only 10-25% of Wenchuan earthquake landslide debris reached river channels within 12 years; 40% remained stored on hillslopes and 35% in colluvial deposits, with full sediment cascade completion projected to require 50-100+ years.

Authors: Chen YG, Lai KY, Lee YH, Suppe J, Chen WS, Lin YNN, Wang Y, Hung JH, Kuo YT
Title: Coseismic fold scarps and their kinematic behavior in the 1999 Chi-Chi earthquake, Taiwan
Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
Parameters: fold scarp geometry, coseismic displacement, fault kinematics
Key_Findings: Coseismic fold scarps along the Chelungpu fault showed 2-8 m of vertical displacement; fault-propagation folding created 15-20° backtilted surfaces that trapped 10⁵-10⁶ m³ of sediment upstream, fundamentally altering local drainage patterns for decades.

Authors: Chen RF, Chan YC, Angelier J, Hu JC, Huang C, Chang KJ, Shih TY
Title: Large earthquake-triggered landslides and mountain belt erosion: The Tsaoling case, Taiwan
Parameters: landslide volume, DEM comparison, erosion rate
Key_Findings: The 1999 Tsaoling landslide mobilized 125×10⁶ m³ of rock creating a 5.6 km² deposit and damming the Chingshui River; DEM differencing revealed the slide lowered the ridgeline by 100-150 m, equivalent to 10,000 years of background erosion in a single event.

Authors: Egholm DL, Knudsen MF, Sandiford M
Title: Lifespan of mountain ranges scaled by feedbacks between landsliding and erosion by rivers
Parameters: mountain range lifespan, erosion feedback, river incision
Key_Findings: Coupled landslide-river models show negative feedbacks limit mountain height to 1500-2500 m in erosive climates; earthquake-triggered landslide sediment reduces river incision capacity by 40-80%, creating a self-regulating mechanism that extends mountain range lifespans by 2-5x.

Authors: Hales TC, Roering JJ
Title: A frost 'buzzsaw' mechanism for erosion of the eastern Southern Alps, New Zealand
Parameters: frost cracking, rockfall rate, erosion mechanism
Key_Findings: Frost cracking intensity peaks at 1800-2200 m elevation in the Southern Alps, generating 2-5 mm/yr of rock-to-sediment conversion; this process preconditions slopes for earthquake-triggered failure by creating fractured rock masses with 30-50% reduced cohesion.

Authors: Robinson TR, Davies TRH
Title: Review article: Potential geomorphic consequences of a future great (Mw = 8.0+) Alpine Fault earthquake, South Island, New Zealand
Journal: Natural Hazards and Earth System Sciences
Parameters: landslide volume prediction, aggradation, river avulsion
Key_Findings: A future Mw8.0+ Alpine Fault earthquake is predicted to trigger 50,000-100,000 landslides totaling 1-10 km³; resultant river aggradation of 10-30 m in major valleys would cause widespread flooding and channel avulsion persisting for decades to centuries.

Authors: Pearce AJ, Watson AJ
Title: Effects of earthquake-induced landslides on sediment budget and transport over a 50-yr period
Parameters: sediment budget, landslide inventory, transport rate
Key_Findings: Sediment budget analysis over 50 years (1935-1985) in the Wairarapa region showed the 1942 Mw7.2 earthquake mobilized ~3.5×10⁶ m³; only 25% reached streams within 10 years while 60% was stored in colluvial hollows still contributing sediment after 40+ years.

Authors: Stark CP, Hovius N
Title: The characterization of landslide size distributions
