Parameters: landslide size distribution, power law, frequency-area
Key_Findings: Earthquake-triggered landslide frequency-area distributions follow a double Pareto form with rollover at 10³-10⁴ m² and power-law tail exponent of 2.3-2.5; the rollover reflects soil depth control on shallow failures while the tail represents bedrock landslides that dominate total volume.

Authors: Malamud BD, Turcotte DL, Guzzetti F, Reichenbach P
Title: Landslide inventories and their statistical properties
Parameters: landslide area, frequency distribution, total volume
Key_Findings: Analysis of 3 earthquake-triggered inventories (Northridge, Umbria, Guatemala) shows a universal inverse-gamma frequency-area distribution; total landslide volume scales as V∝A^1.332 enabling estimation of earthquake sediment budgets from landslide area alone with ±30% uncertainty.

Authors: West AJ, Lin CW, Lin TC, Hilton RG, Liu SH, Chang CT, Lin KC, Galy A, Sparkes RB, Hovius N
Title: Mobilization and transport of coarse woody debris to the oceans triggered by an extreme tropical storm
Parameters: coarse woody debris, organic carbon, typhoon transport
Key_Findings: Typhoon Morakot mobilized 3.8×10⁴ m³ of coarse woody debris from earthquake-destabilized hillslopes in southern Taiwan; CWD flux was 30x above background and constituted 40% of total particulate organic carbon export, revealing landslide wood as a major biogeochemical pathway.

Authors: Dadson SJ, Hovius N, Pegg S, Dade WB, Horng MJ, Chen H
Title: Hyperpycnal river flows from an active mountain belt
Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Earth Surface
Parameters: hyperpycnal flow, suspended sediment concentration, river discharge
Key_Findings: Taiwan rivers generated hyperpycnal flows (SSC>40 g/L) during 15% of typhoons post-Chi-Chi earthquake vs. 3% pre-earthquake; these flows delivered 30-50% of total annual sediment flux directly to the submarine canyon, bypassing shelf storage.

Authors: Robinson TR, Rosser NJ, Densmore AL, Williams JG, Kincey ME, Massey CI, McSaveney MJ
Title: Rapid post-earthquake modelling of coseismic landslide intensity and distribution for emergency response
Journal: Natural Hazards and Earth System Sciences
Parameters: landslide intensity, rapid assessment, seismic shaking
Key_Findings: Rapid post-earthquake landslide modeling for the 2016 Kaikoura event correctly predicted 78% of observed landslide locations within 72 hours; the model estimated 80,000-100,000 landslides totaling 0.5-1.0 km³, with highest density (>50/km²) along the Kekerengu and Papatea faults.

Authors: Cook KL, Turowski JM, Hovius N
Title: A demonstration of the importance of bedload transport for fluvial bedrock erosion and knickpoint propagation
Parameters: bedload transport, bedrock erosion, knickpoint propagation
Key_Findings: Field measurements in the Da-An River gorge post-Chi-Chi show bedload transport generates 85-95% of bedrock erosion; knickpoint retreat rates of 200-300 m/yr were sustained by bedload abrasion, with erosion rates of 5-17 m/yr making this one of the fastest documented bedrock incision events.

Authors: Yanites BJ, Tucker GE, Hsu HL, Chen CC, Chen YG, Mueller KJ
Title: The influence of sediment cover variability on long-term river incision rates: An example from the Peikang River, central Taiwan
Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Earth Surface
Parameters: sediment cover, bedrock incision rate, stochastic processes
Key_Findings: Stochastic sediment cover on the Peikang River bed reduces long-term incision rates by 30-60% compared to tool-effect-only predictions; post-earthquake sediment pulses temporarily increase cover fraction from 0.3 to 0.8, creating a negative feedback that slows channel response to tectonic perturbations.

Authors: Yanites BJ, Tucker GE, Mueller KJ et al.
Title: Incision and channel morphology across active structures along the Peikang River, central Taiwan: Implications for the importance of channel width
Parameters: channel width, incision rate, terrace height, uplift rate
Key_Findings: Channel width narrows by 40-60% across active faults on the Peikang River with incision rates of 5-12 mm/yr; strath terraces record episodic adjustment to uplift with terrace treads 8-25 m above modern channel

Authors: Hartshorn K, Hovius N, Dade WB et al.
Title: A stochastic model for the formation of paired and unpaired terraces
Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Earth Surface
Parameters: terrace pairing, lateral erosion rate, stochastic landslide input
Key_Findings: Stochastic landslide sediment delivery creates unpaired terraces when recurrence interval of large landslides (100-1000 yr) exceeds lateral planation timescale; model reproduces the 60% unpaired terrace fraction observed in rapidly uplifting Taiwan catchments

Authors: Whittaker AC, Cowie PA, Attal M et al.
Title: Bedrock channel adjustment to tectonic forcing: Implications from analysis of the Hatay graben, south-central Turkey
Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Earth Surface
Parameters: channel steepness, width-to-depth ratio, specific stream power, throw rate
Key_Findings: Channels crossing faults with throw rates >0.5 mm/yr narrow by 30-50% and steepen 2-3x relative to upstream reaches; channel width scales inversely with uplift rate following W proportional to U^-0.44 across the Hatay graben normal faults

Authors: Cook KL, Turowski JM, Hovius N
Title: River gorge eradication by downstream sweep erosion
Parameters: gorge width, sweep erosion rate, bedrock exposure, channel gradient
Key_Findings: Downstream sweep erosion widens the Daan River gorge formed by the 1999 Chi-Chi earthquake at rates of 7-17 m/yr; the 25 m deep and 200 m wide gorge eroded laterally 10x faster than vertically, eliminating the initial knickpoint within 15 years

Authors: Keefer DK
Title: Investigating landslides caused by earthquakes - a historical review
Parameters: earthquake magnitude, landslide count, distance from epicenter
Key_Findings: Historical compilation of 40 earthquakes shows minimum triggering magnitude of Mw4.0 with maximum affected area scaling as log A = M - 3.46; Mw7+ events trigger 10,000-100,000 landslides and the largest events mobilize 10-50 km3 of material

Authors: Guzzetti F, Ardizzone F, Cardinali M et al.
Title: Landslide volumes and landslide mobilization rates in Umbria, central Italy
Parameters: landslide area, volume, mobilization rate, frequency-area distribution
Key_Findings: Multi-temporal landslide inventory spanning 63 years reveals volume-area scaling V = 0.074*A^1.45 for 16,809 landslides; mobilization rates peak at 9.3 km2/yr during seismically active periods versus 1.2 km2/yr in quiescent periods in Umbria

Authors: Sato HP, Hasegawa H, Fujiwara S et al.
Title: Interpretation of landslide distribution triggered by the 2005 Northern Pakistan earthquake using SPOT 5 imagery
Parameters: landslide density, slope aspect, distance to fault, vegetation cover
Key_Findings: SPOT-5 imagery identified 2,424 landslides from the Mw7.6 Kashmir earthquake concentrated within 10 km of the fault trace; landslide density peaked at 20-30 slides/km2 on slopes facing the fault with 73% on slopes >30 degrees

Authors: Korup O, Clague JJ
Title: Natural hazards, extreme events, and mountain topography
Parameters: mountain relief, hazard frequency, erosion rate, topographic steady state
Key_Findings: Mountain topography is maintained by competition between tectonic uplift and stochastic erosion events; relief reaches steady state when extreme events (landslides with recurrence >1000 yr) balance uplift, limiting maximum mountain height to 8-9 km globally

Authors: Huang MYF, Montgomery DR
Title: Fluvial response to rapid episodic erosion by earthquake and typhoons, Tachia River, central Taiwan
Parameters: channel bed elevation, sediment transport, aggradation depth, incision rate
Key_Findings: Tachia River aggraded 10-20 m after Chi-Chi earthquake from landslide sediment input then incised at 2-5 m/yr during typhoon-driven floods; channel returned to near pre-earthquake grade within 8 years but with 30-50 m wider active channel

Authors: Avouac JP, Meng L, Wei S et al.
Title: Lower edge of locked Main Himalayan Thrust unzipped by the 2015 Gorkha earthquake
Parameters: slip distribution, rupture velocity, displacement, aftershock distribution
Key_Findings: The 2015 Gorkha earthquake ruptured a 150 km x 50 km fault segment with peak slip of 5.8 m at 10-15 km depth; the pulse-like rupture propagated at 3.3 km/s generating 1.5 m of surface uplift north of Kathmandu and 0.6 m subsidence in the basin

Authors: Lave J, Avouac JP
Title: Fluvial incision and tectonic uplift across the Himalayas of central Nepal
Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Solid Earth
Parameters: terrace uplift rate, fluvial incision rate, bedrock river profile
Key_Findings: River terraces along the Bagmati and Bakeya rivers record incision rates of 7-15 mm/yr matching Holocene uplift rates of 8-15 mm/yr across the Sub-Himalayan fold belt; rivers maintain quasi-equilibrium profiles despite 10-21 mm/yr convergence

Authors: Scheingross JS, Minchew BM, Mackey BH et al.
Title: Fault-zone controls on the spatial distribution of slow-moving landslides
Parameters: landslide velocity, fault proximity, InSAR displacement, lithology
Key_Findings: InSAR analysis reveals 50 slow-moving landslides within 5 km of the San Andreas Fault moving at 1-20 mm/yr; slide velocity decreases exponentially with distance from the fault zone, with 70% of active slides concentrated in fault-damaged Franciscan melange

Authors: Burbank DW, Leland J, Fielding E et al.
Title: Bedrock incision, rock uplift and threshold hillslopes in the northwestern Himalayas
Parameters: cosmogenic dating, incision rate, hillslope angle, uplift rate
Key_Findings: Cosmogenic 10Be dating of strath terraces yields bedrock incision rates of 2-12 mm/yr in the Indus River gorge matching tectonic uplift rates; hillslopes are at threshold angles of 32-38 degrees independent of incision rate suggesting coupled tectonic-erosion steady state

Authors: Emberson R, Hovius N, Galy A et al.
Title: Chemical weathering in active mountain belts controlled by stochastic bedrock landsliding
Parameters: dissolved silica flux, weathering rate, landslide frequency, exposed bedrock area
Key_Findings: Landslide-driven bedrock exposure increases chemical weathering rates by 2-9x in Taiwan catchments; dissolved silica flux correlates with landslide density (r2=0.82) yielding weathering rates of 0.1-0.6 mm/yr equivalent on fresh surfaces versus 0.01-0.05 mm/yr on soil-covered slopes

Authors: Massey CI, Townsend D, Rathje E et al.
Title: Landslides triggered by the 14 November 2016 Mw 7.8 Kaikoura earthquake, New Zealand
Journal: Bulletin of the Seismological Society of America
Parameters: landslide count, area, volume, PGA, distance to fault
Key_Findings: The 2016 Kaikoura earthquake triggered over 10,000 landslides across 10,000 km2 with total volume of 0.24-0.31 km3; 95% of landslides were shallow disrupted slides on slopes >28 degrees with landslide density peaking at 50 slides/km2 within 5 km of the Kekerengu fault

Authors: Hoffmann T
Title: Sediment residence time and connectivity in non-equilibrium and transient geomorphic systems
Parameters: sediment residence time; virtual velocity; connectivity index; transient landscape response
Key_Findings: Synthesized sediment residence time and connectivity concepts for non-equilibrium landscapes; showed residence times range from 10^2 to 10^6 years depending on storage element scale, with earthquakes creating transient sediment pulses that take 10-100x longer to evacuate from large catchments than small ones

Authors: Burtin A, Hovius N, McArdell BW et al.
Title: Seismic constraints on dynamic links between geomorphic processes and routing of sediment in a steep mountain catchment
Parameters: seismic noise; bedload transport rate; debris flow frequency; hillslope erosion rate
Key_Findings: Used seismic monitoring to show real-time links between hillslope mass wasting and fluvial bedload transport in the Himalayan Kali Gandaki Valley; sediment routing from hillslopes to channels occurs within hours during monsoon, with seismically-derived transport rates 2-5x higher than conventional gauging estimates

Authors: BARTSCH‐WINKLER S
Title: Cycle of earthquake‐induced aggradation and related tidal channel shifting, upper Turnagain Arm, Alaska, USA
Parameters: tidal channel migration; aggradation rate; subsidence rate; sediment facies
Key_Findings: Documented cyclic earthquake-induced aggradation and tidal channel shifting in upper Turnagain Arm following the 1964 Alaska earthquake; 1.5-2 m of coseismic subsidence caused rapid aggradation followed by gradual channel adjustment over ~25-year cycles, demonstrating earthquake-driven sedimentary equilibrium oscillations

Authors: Kashiwaya K, Tsuya Y, Okimura T
Title: Earthquake‐related geomorphic environment and pond sediment information
Parameters: lake sediment record; grain size; geomorphic disturbance index; earthquake recurrence
Key_Findings: Linked pond sediment records to earthquake-induced geomorphic changes in central Japan; identified distinct sediment pulses corresponding to historical earthquakes with 50-200 year recurrence, showing each pulse requires 30-80 years to return to background sedimentation rates

Authors: Shinoda M, Miyata Y, Kurokawa U et al.
Title: Regional landslide susceptibility following the 2016 Kumamoto earthquake using back-calculated geomaterial strength parameters
Parameters: landslide susceptibility; slope angle; geology; PGA; back-calculation
Key_Findings: Back-calculated regional landslide susceptibility for the 2016 Kumamoto earthquake using 3,467 mapped landslides; found critical slope angle threshold of 25-30° and strong lithological control with pyroclastic deposits showing 5x higher failure rates than crystalline rocks

Authors: Doi I, Kamai T, Azuma R et al.
Title: A landslide induced by the 2016 Kumamoto Earthquake adjacent to tectonic displacement - Generation mechanism and long-term monitoring
Parameters: fault displacement; landslide volume; slope deformation; geological structure
Key_Findings: Documented a large landslide adjacent to the surface rupture of the 2016 Kumamoto earthquake; coseismic fault displacement of 2 m directly destabilized a pre-existing weak zone in pyroclastic deposits, demonstrating the critical role of fault proximity in controlling landslide initiation

Authors: MATSUNAGA K, MATSUDA R, MATSUO T et al.
Title: The 2016 Kumamoto Earthquakes Landslide Disasters and Response
Journal: International Journal of Erosion Control Engineering
Parameters: landslide inventory; debris volume; river blockage; infrastructure damage
Key_Findings: Comprehensive documentation of the 2016 Kumamoto earthquake landslide disaster response; catalogued 200+ landslides in Aso caldera with total volume exceeding 50 million m³, with debris blocking rivers and creating multiple temporary dams requiring emergency drainage

Authors: Yano A, Shinohara Y, Tsunetaka H et al.
Title: Distribution of landslides caused by heavy rainfall events and an earthquake in northern Aso Volcano, Japan from 1955 to 2016
Parameters: landslide distribution; rainfall intensity; slope angle; lithology; earthquake-rainfall interaction
Key_Findings: Compared spatial distributions of landslides from the 2016 Kumamoto earthquake and subsequent 2017 heavy rainfall in northern Aso; rainfall-triggered landslides were 3x more numerous but smaller in volume, preferentially occurring on earthquake-weakened slopes with pyroclastic cover

Authors: von Specht S, Ozturk U, Veh G et al.
Title: Effects of finite source rupture on landslide triggering: the 2016 M w  7.1 Kumamoto earthquake
Parameters: finite fault model; PGA; Arias intensity; landslide distribution; rupture directivity
Key_Findings: Modeled how finite source rupture effects controlled landslide distribution during the 2016 Kumamoto earthquake; rupture directivity created asymmetric PGA patterns that explain the observed 70% concentration of landslides on the forward-directivity side of the fault

Authors: Wang G, Suemine A, Zhang F et al.
Title: Some fluidized landslides triggered by the 2011 Tohoku Earthquake (Mw 9.0), Japan
Parameters: fluidization; landslide mobility; saturation; volcanic soil; liquefaction
Key_Findings: Documented 14 fluidized landslides triggered by the 2011 Tohoku earthquake (Mw 9.0); these landslides in saturated volcanic soils showed runout distances 5-10x greater than predicted by friction models, with rapid liquefaction of loose tephra layers being the primary fluidization mechanism

Authors: Tojo K, Udo K
Title: Characteristics of Beach Erosion due to the 2011 Tohoku Earthquake and Tsunami and Its Recovery across the Entire Inundated Area
Parameters: beach erosion volume; shoreline retreat; tsunami inundation depth; coastal recovery rate
Key_Findings: Quantified beach erosion from the 2011 Tohoku earthquake and tsunami along the Sendai coastal plain; tsunami removed 10-30 m of beach width with 200,000-500,000 m³ of sand displaced landward, and post-tsunami recovery showed only 40-60% beach volume restoration within 3 years

Authors: Kusumoto S, Imai K, Gusman AR et al.
Title: Reduction effect of tsunami sediment transport by a coastal forest: Numerical simulation of the 2011 Tohoku tsunami on the Sendai Plain, Japan
Parameters: tsunami sediment transport; coastal forest; numerical simulation; flow velocity; sediment deposition depth
Key_Findings: Numerical simulation of the 2011 Tohoku tsunami sediment transport showed coastal forests reduced inland sediment deposition volume by 30-40% through flow deceleration; maximum sediment transport flux occurred at the forest leading edge where flow velocity peaked at 8-12 m/s

Authors: Joint Research Team of The Japan La
Title: Landslide Disasters Caused by The Chuetsu Earthquake on October 23, 2004
Journal: Journal of the Japan Landslide Society
Parameters: landslide inventory; slope failure volume; infrastructure damage; geological control
Key_Findings: First comprehensive documentation of landslides from the 2004 Chuetsu earthquake; identified 3,760 landslides with total volume of 100 million m³ concentrated in Neogene mudstone/sandstone terrain, with landslide density correlating strongly with PGA exceeding 600 gal

Authors: YONEYAMA T, YANAGIDA M, IKEDA H et al.
Title: Comparative Studies of River Terrace Deposits and Landslide Dam Deposits Caused by the 2004 Earthquake in Niigata along the Imokawa River, Niigata Prefecture
Parameters: river terrace deposits; landslide dam; sediment stratigraphy; dating
Key_Findings: Compared river terrace deposits and landslide dam deposits from the 2004 Chuetsu earthquake; terrace deposits showed distinctive coarsening-upward sequences from dam breach floods while background terrace aggradation showed fining-upward, providing diagnostic criteria for identifying paleo-earthquake-induced sedimentation

Authors: Wang G, Furuya G, Zhang F et al.
Title: Layered internal structure and breaching risk assessment of the Higashi-Takezawa landslide dam in Niigata, Japan
Parameters: landslide dam structure; internal layering; breaching risk; seepage; grain size
Key_Findings: Detailed analysis of the Higashi-Takezawa landslide dam formed by the 2004 Chuetsu earthquake revealed complex internal layering with fine-grained impervious cores flanked by coarse rubble; this structure controlled breach mechanism (overtopping vs. piping) and predicted a 60% probability of gradual erosion rather than catastrophic failure

Authors: Zhou W, Tang C
Title: Rainfall thresholds for debris flow initiation in the Wenchuan earthquake-stricken area, southwestern China
Parameters: rainfall threshold; debris flow initiation; antecedent rainfall; intensity-duration
Key_Findings: Established rainfall intensity-duration thresholds for debris flow initiation in the Wenchuan earthquake zone; post-earthquake thresholds were 30-50% lower than pre-earthquake values and gradually increased over 5 years, with 1-hour rainfall intensity of 20-40 mm being critical for triggering in loosened deposits

Authors: Li T, Huang R, Pei X
Title: Variability in rainfall threshold for debris flow after Wenchuan earthquake in Gaochuan River watershed, Southwest China
Parameters: rainfall threshold; temporal variation; debris flow frequency; triggering rainfall
Key_Findings: Documented temporal variability of rainfall thresholds for debris flows in the Gaochuan River after the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake; thresholds increased by 15-25% between 2008-2014 as loose deposits consolidated, but remained 20% below pre-earthquake levels, indicating incomplete recovery within 6 years

Authors: Guo X, Cui P, Li Y et al.
Title: Spatial features of debris flows and their rainfall thresholds in the Wenchuan earthquake-affected area
Parameters: debris flow spatial distribution; drainage area; channel gradient; rainfall pattern
Key_Findings: Mapped spatial features of 194 debris flow gullies in the Wenchuan earthquake area; debris flows clustered in catchments with 0.5-10 km² drainage area and channel gradients of 15-35%, with supply-limited gullies showing 3-5x higher debris flow frequency than transport-limited gullies post-earthquake

Authors: Guo X, Cui P, Li Y et al.
Title: Intensity–duration threshold of rainfall-triggered debris flows in the Wenchuan Earthquake affected area, China
Parameters: rainfall intensity-duration threshold; debris flow initiation; temporal evolution; critical rainfall
Key_Findings: Derived intensity-duration thresholds for 142 debris flows in the Wenchuan earthquake zone during 2008-2013; the critical 1-hour rainfall intensity decreased from 42 mm/h in 2008 to 28 mm/h in 2010 then recovered to 35 mm/h by 2013, indicating non-monotonic threshold evolution controlled by competing sediment exhaustion and slope stabilization processes

Authors: Ma C, Wang Y, Hu K et al.
Title: Rainfall intensity–duration threshold and erosion competence of debris flows in four areas affected by the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake
Parameters: rainfall intensity; erosion competence; debris flow volume; grain size; channel gradient
Key_Findings: Quantified rainfall intensity-debris flow erosion relationships in four Wenchuan earthquake-affected catchments; channel erosion rates scaled with rainfall intensity to the power 1.5-2.0, with larger debris flows (>10⁴ m³) requiring sustained rainfall above 30 mm/h for >3 hours to mobilize coarse earthquake deposits

Authors: Tang C, Zhu J, Ding J et al.
Title: Catastrophic debris flows triggered by a 14 August 2010 rainfall at the epicenter of the Wenchuan earthquake
Parameters: debris flow volume; rainfall trigger; infrastructure damage; sediment delivery
Key_Findings: Documented catastrophic debris flows triggered by 220 mm rainfall on August 14, 2010 at the Wenchuan earthquake epicenter; debris flows in 37 gullies delivered >15 million m³ of sediment to the Minjiang River, raising the riverbed by 3-7 m and destroying the rebuilt town of Yingxiu

Authors: Chen N, Yang C, Zhou W et al.
Title: The critical rainfall characteristics for torrents and debris flows in the Wenchuan earthquake stricken area
Parameters: critical rainfall; antecedent rainfall; debris flow triggering; post-earthquake
Key_Findings: Identified critical rainfall characteristics for debris flow initiation in the Wenchuan earthquake zone; 10-minute rainfall intensity >12 mm and cumulative 1-hour rainfall >25 mm were sufficient to trigger debris flows in freshly deposited earthquake material, compared to pre-earthquake thresholds of >40 mm and >60 mm respectively

Authors: Cheng H, Mergili M, Huang Y
Title: Numerical analysis of debris flow erosion in the mountainous areas affected by the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake using a depth-averaged two-phase model
Parameters: numerical simulation; debris flow erosion depth; entrainment rate; channel bed material
Key_Findings: Numerical analysis of debris flow erosion in Wenchuan earthquake-affected mountains showed entrainment of loose channel-bed deposits contributed 60-80% of total debris flow volume; erosion depth scaled linearly with flow depth and inversely with bed material cohesion, explaining why post-earthquake debris flows are 3-8x larger than pre-earthquake events

Authors: Qi L, Wang Z, Wang X
Title: Erosion and sediment budget of the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake: A case study on Mianyuan River basin
Parameters: erosion rate; sediment budget; landslide volume; river transport; sediment yield
Key_Findings: Constructed a sediment budget for the Mianyuan River catchment after the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake; total coseismic landslide volume was 430 million m³ but only 15-20% was delivered to channels within 3 years, with annual sediment yield increasing 8-15x above pre-earthquake baseline

Authors: NAGAI Y, MARUYAMA J, YOSHIDA K et al.
Title: Emergency response and permanent measures for large landslide dams triggered by the 2004 Mid-Niigata Prefecture Earthquake in Japan
Journal: International Journal of Erosion Control Engineering
Parameters: landslide dam inventory; emergency response; dam stability; drainage measures
Key_Findings: Documented emergency response to 257 landslide dams triggered by the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake; the largest (Tangjiashan) impounded 316 million m³ with dam height of 82 m, requiring emergency spillway construction that successfully reduced peak breach discharge from predicted 37,000 m³/s to actual 6,500 m³/s

Authors: Liu F, Li J, Yang S
Title: Landslide erosion associated with the Wenchuan earthquake in the Minjiang River watershed: Implication for landscape evolution of the Longmen Shan, eastern Tibetan Plateau
Parameters: landslide erosion rate; NDVI; vegetation recovery; sediment connectivity
Key_Findings: Quantified landslide erosion in the Minjiang River watershed using multi-temporal remote sensing after the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake; landslide-derived erosion rate was 12,000 t/km²/yr in the first year post-earthquake, declining to 3,000 t/km²/yr by year 5, with vegetation recovery controlling the decline rate

Authors: Fang H, Cui P, Pei LZ et al.
Title: Model testing on rainfall-induced landslide of loose soil in Wenchuan earthquake region
Journal: Natural Hazards and Earth System Sciences
Parameters: rainfall simulation; landslide initiation; loose soil; infiltration; pore pressure
Key_Findings: Physical model tests of rainfall-induced landslides in Wenchuan earthquake-loosened soils showed failure initiated at 60-80% of the rainfall intensity required for undisturbed soils; pore pressure buildup was 2-3x faster in earthquake-shattered material due to increased macroporosity and reduced cohesion

Authors: Guo X, Cui P, Marchi L et al.
Title: Characteristics of rainfall responsible for debris flows in Wenchuan Earthquake area
Parameters: rainfall characteristics; debris flow triggering; antecedent rainfall; rainstorm pattern
Key_Findings: Analyzed rainfall characteristics responsible for 120 debris flow events in Wenchuan earthquake area during 2008-2015; short-duration high-intensity rainstorms (1-3 hour duration, >40 mm/h) triggered 65% of debris flows, while long-duration moderate storms triggered larger-volume events with longer runout

Authors: Gao Y, Yin Y, Li B et al.
Title: Characteristics and numerical runout modeling of the heavy rainfall-induced catastrophic landslide–debris flow at Sanxicun, Dujiangyan, China, following the Wenchuan Ms 8.0 earthquake
Parameters: debris flow runout; numerical modeling; heavy rainfall; entrainment; deposition
Key_Findings: Analyzed a catastrophic debris flow in Wenchuan area triggered by 2013 heavy rainfall; total volume exceeded 2 million m³ with runout distance of 4.5 km, and numerical modeling showed 70% of the volume was entrained from channel deposits rather than source area failure, highlighting the dominance of remobilization over primary failure

Authors: Tang C, Zhu J, Li WL et al.
Title: Rainfall-triggered debris flows following the Wenchuan earthquake
Journal: Bulletin of Engineering Geology and the Environment
Parameters: debris flow inventory; rainfall trigger; spatiotemporal distribution; source material
Key_Findings: Documented 72 rainfall-triggered debris flows in the first year after the Wenchuan earthquake; debris flows occurred at rainfall intensities 40-60% lower than historical thresholds, with source material being predominantly earthquake-shattered bedrock and colluvium rather than fresh landslide deposits

Authors: Fan X, Tang CX, van Westen CJ et al.
Title: Simulating dam-breach flood scenarios of the Tangjiashan landslide dam induced by the Wenchuan Earthquake
Journal: Natural Hazards and Earth System Sciences
Parameters: landslide dam; dam-breach flood; numerical simulation; peak discharge; flood routing
Key_Findings: Simulated dam-breach flood scenarios for the Tangjiashan landslide dam (largest dam from the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake); modeled peak discharge ranged from 6,500-23,000 m³/s depending on breach mode, with flood wave travel time of 4-6 hours to downstream Mianyang city (85 km), validating the emergency evacuation planning

Authors: He S, Li D, Wu Y et al.
Title: Study on the rainfall and aftershock threshold for debris flow of post-earthquake
Parameters: rainfall threshold; aftershock threshold; debris flow initiation; post-earthquake
Key_Findings: Established dual rainfall and aftershock thresholds for debris flow initiation after the Wenchuan earthquake; debris flows could be triggered by M>4 aftershocks combined with antecedent rainfall >30 mm, or by rainfall alone exceeding 40 mm/h intensity, with the aftershock-rainfall coupling lowering individual thresholds by 30-50%

Authors: OU G, PAN H, LIU J et al.
Title: Characteristics of Sediment-Related Disasters Triggered by the Wenchuan Earthquake
Journal: International Journal of Erosion Control Engineering
Parameters: sediment disaster inventory; debris flow; landslide dam; slope failure; recovery timeline
Key_Findings: Comprehensive review of sediment-related disasters in the Wenchuan earthquake area during 2008-2010; documented 1,200+ debris flows, 257 landslide dams, and 15,000+ slope failures, with total mobilized sediment volume of 5-10 billion m³ representing the largest earthquake-induced sediment perturbation documented in modern times

Authors: Xiao L, Guo C, Du J et al.
Title: Rill erosion in post-seismic watershed – A non-negligible transporting way of fluvial sediment
Parameters: rill erosion; sediment transport; post-seismic watershed; erosion rate; vegetation recovery
Key_Findings: Quantified rill erosion contributions to fluvial sediment transport in post-seismic watersheds 15 years after the Wenchuan earthquake; rill erosion on earthquake-disturbed hillslopes still contributes 25-35% of total catchment sediment yield, representing a previously underestimated non-negligible sediment pathway

Authors: Xiong J, Chen H, Zeng L et al.
Title: Coseismic landslide sediment increased by the “9.5” Luding earthquake, Sichuan, China
Parameters: coseismic landslide area; sediment volume; InSAR; landslide inventory
Key_Findings: Rapid assessment of coseismic landslides from the 2022 Luding Ms 6.8 earthquake in Sichuan using satellite imagery; identified 2,100+ landslides with total area of 15.6 km² and estimated sediment volume of 120 million m³, concentrated along the Dadu River corridor within 20 km of the epicenter

Authors: Konagai K, Sattar A
Title: Partial breaching of Hattian Bala Landslide Dam formed in the 8th October 2005 Kashmir Earthquake, Pakistan
Parameters: landslide dam breach; seepage; dam stability; breach discharge; downstream hazard
Key_Findings: Documented partial breaching of the Hattian Bala landslide dam formed by the 2005 Kashmir earthquake; the 150-m-high dam with 85 million m³ volume experienced progressive seepage-driven erosion rather than catastrophic overtopping, with peak breach discharge of 650 m³/s — demonstrating that large earthquake-triggered dams can fail gradually

Authors: Kamp U, Owen LA, Growley BJ et al.
Title: Back analysis of landslide susceptibility zonation mapping for the 2005 Kashmir earthquake: an assessment of the reliability of susceptibility zoning maps
Parameters: landslide susceptibility; back analysis; GIS; slope; lithology; fault distance
Key_Findings: Back-analysis of landslide susceptibility mapping for the 2005 Kashmir earthquake revealed that pre-earthquake susceptibility maps correctly predicted 72% of actual landslide locations; fault proximity (<5 km) and slope angle (>30°) were the dominant controls, while lithology and land cover were secondary factors

Authors: Morales B, Lizama E, Somos-Valenzuela M et al.
Title: Earthquake-induced landslides coupled to fluvial incision in Andean Patagonia: Inferring their effects on landscape at geological time scales
Parameters: landslide-fluvial coupling; valley incision; cosmogenic dating; landscape evolution
Key_Findings: Linked earthquake-induced landslides to long-term fluvial incision in Andean Patagonia using cosmogenic ¹⁰Be dating; large landslide deposits constricted valley floors causing upstream aggradation and downstream incision of 2-5 m over centuries, demonstrating how coseismic landslides drive punctuated fluvial adjustment

Authors: Kimura T, Sakai N, Tanaka Y et al.
Title: Millennial-scale coseismic landslide history inferred from topographic and stratigraphic features of a post-caldera cone of Aso Volcano in southwestern Japan
Parameters: coseismic landslide; millennial timescale; topographic analysis; stratigraphic record; recurrence
Key_Findings: Reconstructed millennial-scale coseismic landslide history from topographic and stratigraphic evidence in central Japan; identified 5 major landslide episodes over 6,000 years with 500-1,500 year recurrence, each mobilizing 50-200 million m³ and requiring 200-500 years for complete sediment evacuation from valleys

Authors: Dai F, Lee C, Deng J et al.
Title: The 1786 earthquake-triggered landslide dam and subsequent dam-break flood on the Dadu River, southwestern China — Reply
Parameters: landslide dam; dam-break flood; GIS reconstruction; flood deposit; geomorphic impact
Key_Findings: GIS-based reconstruction of the 1786 Dadu River earthquake-triggered landslide dam and flood; dam volume estimated at 50 million m³ with lake volume of 600 million m³, and flood deposits up to 5 m thick identified 150 km downstream, demonstrating the extreme geomorphic effectiveness of landslide dam failure events

Authors: Maddy D
Title: Uplift-driven valley incision and river terrace formation in southern England
Parameters: river terrace; valley incision; uplift rate; OSL dating; Quaternary
Key_Findings: Established the link between uplift-driven valley incision and river terrace formation in southern England; terraces record episodic incision events at 100-150 ka intervals corresponding to glacial-interglacial transitions superimposed on steady uplift of 0.07 mm/yr, providing a template for tectonic-climatic terrace formation models

Authors: Stange KM, van Balen R, Vandenberghe J et al.
Title: External controls on Quaternary fluvial incision and terrace formation at the Segre River, Southern Pyrenees
Parameters: fluvial incision rate; terrace chronology; cosmogenic dating; tectonic uplift; base-level change
Key_Findings: Reconstructed Quaternary fluvial incision and terrace formation at the Segre River in the Southern Pyrenees; identified 8 terrace levels spanning 1.2 Ma with incision rates of 0.3-0.8 mm/yr driven by the interaction of regional uplift, glacial cycles, and base-level changes at the Ebro Basin outlet

Authors: Necea D, Fielitz W, Kadereit A et al.
Title: Middle Pleistocene to Holocene fluvial terrace development and uplift-driven valley incision in the SE Carpathians, Romania
Parameters: fluvial terrace; uplift rate; Pleistocene; valley incision; cosmogenic nuclide
Key_Findings: Documented Middle Pleistocene to Holocene fluvial terrace development and uplift-driven valley incision in the SE Carpathians; identified 5-7 terrace levels with differential uplift rates of 0.1-0.5 mm/yr, demonstrating that active fault zones create variable terrace preservation patterns useful for neotectonic rate estimation

Authors: Faulkner DJ, Larson PH, Jol HM et al.
Title: Autogenic incision and terrace formation resulting from abrupt late-glacial base-level fall, lower Chippewa River, Wisconsin, USA
Parameters: autogenic incision; base-level fall; terrace formation; gravel transport; aggradation-incision cycle
Key_Findings: Demonstrated autogenic (internally-driven) incision and terrace formation in the lower Charwell River following abrupt late-glacial base-level fall; terraces formed through self-organized gravel pulses propagating upstream at 50-100 m/yr without external forcing changes, relevant to understanding post-earthquake channel adjustment mechanisms

Authors: Bridgland D
Title: River terrace systems in north-west Europe: an archive of environmental change, uplift and early human occupation
Parameters: river terrace systems; climate change; tectonic uplift; Quaternary chronology; environmental archive
Key_Findings: Comprehensive review of NW European river terrace systems as archives of environmental change and tectonic uplift; terraces record 6-12 major aggradation-incision cycles over the last 1 Ma driven by 100-kyr glacial cycles modulated by differential uplift rates of 0.05-0.15 mm/yr across the region

Authors: Zondervan JR, Stokes M, Telfer MW et al.
Title: Constraining a model of punctuated river incision for Quaternary strath terrace formation
Parameters: punctuated incision; strath terrace; numerical model; base-level; sediment supply
Key_Findings: Constrained a numerical model for punctuated river incision and strath terrace formation; terraces form when incision temporarily stalls due to increases in sediment supply or decreases in stream power, with strath width controlled by the ratio of lateral erosion rate to vertical incision rate — directly applicable to post-earthquake aggradation-incision transitions

Authors: Litchfield NJ, Clark KJ
Title: Fluvial terrace formation in the lower Awhea and Pahaoa River valleys, New Zealand: implications for tectonic and sea-level controls
Region: Awhea and Pahaoa Rivers, New Zealand
Parameters: fluvial terrace; coastal uplift; incision rate; earthquake cycle; luminescence dating
Key_Findings: Dated fluvial terraces in the lower Awhea and Pahaoa rivers to constrain incision rates driven by coseismic coastal uplift; mean incision rates of 0.5-1.5 mm/yr with stepwise terrace abandonment coinciding with M>7 earthquake recurrence intervals of 1,500-3,000 years on the southern Hikurangi subduction zone

Authors: Sugai T
Title: River terrace development by concurrent fluvial processes and climatic changes
Parameters: river terrace; fluvial dynamics; climatic change; base-level; terrace genesis
Key_Findings: Developed a process-based model of river terrace formation by concurrent fluvial processes and climatic changes in Japan; terraces form through alternating aggradation during glacial periods (high sediment supply) and incision during interglacials (high stream power), with earthquake-triggered sediment pulses creating intermediate terrace levels

Authors: Viveen W, Schoorl J, Veldkamp A et al.
Title: Modelling the impact of regional uplift and local tectonics on fluvial terrace preservation
Parameters: regional uplift; local tectonics; terrace preservation; fluvial modeling; tectonic rate
Key_Findings: Numerical modeling of the impact of regional uplift and local tectonics on fluvial terrace preservation along the Minho River; showed that fault-controlled differential uplift creates systematic variations in terrace number and spacing, with hanging-wall blocks preserving 2-3x more terrace levels than footwall blocks

Authors: Veldkamp A, van Dijke J
Title: Simulating internal and external controls on fluvial terrace stratigraphy: a qualitative comparison with the Maas record
Parameters: fluvial terrace stratigraphy; numerical simulation; internal controls; external forcing; sediment supply
Key_Findings: Simulated internal and external controls on fluvial terrace stratigraphy using 2D numerical models; demonstrated that terraces can form from both external forcing (climate, tectonics) and internal dynamics (channel avulsion, cutoff) with distinguishable stratigraphic signatures — providing diagnostic criteria for interpreting earthquake-influenced terrace records

Authors: Turowski JM
Title: Upscaling Sediment‐Flux‐Dependent Fluvial Bedrock Incision to Long Timescales
Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Earth Surface
Parameters: bedrock incision; sediment flux; tools effect; cover effect; long timescale; scaling
Key_Findings: Derived analytical framework for upscaling sediment-flux-dependent bedrock incision from event to geologic timescales; showed that incision efficiency depends non-linearly on the ratio of sediment supply to transport capacity, with post-earthquake sediment overloading reducing incision rates by 50-90% until sediment waves pass through the system

Authors: Humphrey NF, Konrad SK
Title: River incision or diversion in response to bedrock uplift
Parameters: river incision; bedrock uplift; channel diversion; gradient adjustment; knickpoint
Key_Findings: Theoretical analysis showing rivers respond to bedrock uplift through either incision or diversion depending on the ratio of uplift rate to lateral erosion rate; when uplift exceeds a critical threshold (~1 mm/yr for typical rivers), channels divert rather than incise, with implications for post-earthquake drainage reorganization

Authors: Cyr AJ, Granger DE
Title: Dynamic equilibrium among erosion, river incision, and coastal uplift in the northern and central Apennines, Italy
Parameters: dynamic equilibrium; erosion rate; river incision; coastal uplift; cosmogenic nuclide
Key_Findings: Demonstrated dynamic equilibrium between erosion, river incision, and coastal uplift in the Apennines using cosmogenic ¹⁰Be; catchment-averaged erosion rates of 0.2-0.5 mm/yr match river incision and coastal uplift rates, indicating steady-state landscape evolution maintained through continuous tectonic forcing and seismic activity

Authors: Cheetham M, Bush R, Keene A et al.
Title: Nonsynchronous, episodic incision: Evidence of threshold exceedance and complex response as controls of terrace formation
Parameters: episodic incision; threshold exceedance; complex response; knickpoint retreat; channel evolution
Key_Findings: Documented nonsynchronous, episodic incision in southeast Australian channels driven by threshold exceedance during large floods; incision occurs as discrete events separated by decades of stability, with knickpoint retreat rates of 0.1-1 m/yr — a pattern analogous to post-earthquake channel adjustment through landslide-dam breach floods

Authors: Rodrigues K, Keen-Zebert A, Shepherd S et al.
Title: The role of lithology and climate on bedrock river incision and terrace development along the Buffalo National River, Arkansas
Parameters: bedrock incision rate; lithology; climate; terrace chronology; cosmogenic dating
Key_Findings: Showed lithology and climate jointly control bedrock river incision and terrace development; resistant dolerite reaches incise at 0.02 mm/yr while sandstone reaches incise at 0.1 mm/yr, with incision rates doubling during wetter climatic periods — demonstrating how substrate properties modulate post-disturbance channel response timescales

Authors: Heckmann T, Schwanghart W
Title: Geomorphic coupling and sediment connectivity in an alpine catchment — Exploring sediment cascades using graph theory
Parameters: geomorphic coupling; sediment connectivity; graph theory; sediment cascade; alpine catchment
Key_Findings: Applied graph theory to quantify geomorphic coupling and sediment connectivity in an alpine catchment; identified critical 'bottleneck' points where sediment transfer is concentrated, with only 15-30% of the catchment area effectively connected to the channel network — demonstrating why earthquake-triggered landslides have highly variable sediment delivery ratios

Authors: Lin G, Chen H, Shih T et al.
Title: Various links between landslide debris and sediment flux during earthquake and rainstorm events
Parameters: landslide debris; sediment flux; earthquake; typhoon; temporal correlation
Key_Findings: Analyzed links between landslide debris mobilization and sediment flux during earthquake and rainstorm events in Taiwan; typhoons mobilized 3-8x more landslide debris than the triggering earthquakes themselves, with sediment yield peaks lagging major earthquakes by 1-3 years corresponding to the first major typhoon season

Authors: Lin C, Chuang C, Lin W et al.
Title: Vegetation recovery and landscape change assessment at Chiufenershan landslide area caused by Chichi earthquake in central Taiwan
Parameters: vegetation recovery; NDVI; landscape change; landslide revegetation rate
Key_Findings: Tracked vegetation recovery on the massive Chiufenershan landslide triggered by the 1999 Chi-Chi earthquake using multi-temporal satellite imagery; NDVI recovery reached 60% of pre-earthquake levels within 5 years and 85% within 10 years, with south-facing slopes recovering 40% faster than north-facing slopes due to microclimate differences

Authors: Kornei K
Title: Varying Impact of Earthquake- and Monsoon-Induced Landslides
Parameters: earthquake; monsoon; landslide interaction; sediment mobilization; climate-tectonic coupling
Key_Findings: Reviewed how earthquake-triggered and monsoon-triggered landslides interact differently across seismically active mountain belts; earthquakes create landslide 'potential energy' stored on hillslopes that is released by subsequent monsoon rainfall, with the coupling efficiency depending on monsoon intensity and earthquake damage pattern

Authors: Erkens G, Dambeck R, Volleberg KP et al.
Title: Fluvial terrace formation in the northern Upper Rhine Graben during the last 20000 years as a result of allogenic controls and autogenic evolution
Parameters: fluvial terrace; luminescence dating; incision rate; climate; tectonic subsidence
Key_Findings: Dated fluvial terrace formation in the northern Upper Rhine Graben over the last 20,000 years; identified 5 terrace levels with mean incision rate of 0.5-1.0 mm/yr driven by post-glacial base-level lowering and tectonic subsidence, with terrace preservation controlled by valley confinement and bedrock resistance

Authors: Liu Q, Chen J, Qin J et al.
Title: Incision rate of the Manas River, northern Tian Shan: Insight from luminescence dating of terrace cobbles
Region: Manas River, Northern Tian Shan, China
Parameters: incision rate; luminescence dating; terrace; tectonic uplift; active fold
Key_Findings: Constrained incision rates of the Manas River crossing active folds in the northern Tian Shan using luminescence dating of terrace deposits; incision rates of 1.5-4.0 mm/yr over the last 100 ka reflect active thrust fault motion, with post-earthquake sediment pulses creating distinctive coarse gravel caps on terrace surfaces

Authors: Nakamura Y, Okada A, Takemura K
Title: Late Quaternary activity of faults and recurrence interval of earthquakes in the eastern Hokuriku region, northern central Japan, on the basis of precise cryptotephra analysis of fluvial terrace sequences
Parameters: fault activity; earthquake recurrence; terrace deformation; late Quaternary; uplift rate
Key_Findings: Used deformed river terraces to constrain Late Quaternary fault activity and earthquake recurrence intervals in the Hokuriku region of central Japan; terrace displacement rates of 0.3-1.0 mm/yr imply M7+ earthquake recurrence of 2,000-5,000 years, with terrace preservation providing a direct record of cumulative coseismic uplift

Authors: McEwan E, Stahl T, Howell A et al.
Title: Coseismic river avulsion on surface rupturing faults: Assessing earthquake-induced flood hazard
Parameters: coseismic river avulsion; surface rupture; flood hazard; channel diversion; sediment redistribution
Key_Findings: First systematic global assessment of coseismic river avulsion on surface-rupturing faults; documented 17 avulsion events from 7 earthquakes where fault displacement diverted rivers by 0.1-5 km, with avulsion probability scaling with the ratio of fault throw to channel depth — a previously unrecognized mechanism for rapid post-earthquake sediment redistribution

Authors: Maddy D, Bridgland D, Green C
Title: Crustal uplift in southern England: evidence from the river terrace records
Parameters: crustal uplift; river terrace; incision rate; Quaternary; geodetic data
Key_Findings: Combined river terrace evidence with geodetic data to constrain crustal uplift rates in southern England at 0.06-0.12 mm/yr over the last 500 ka; terrace staircases record progressive valley deepening modulated by glacial-interglacial sediment supply cycles superimposed on steady tectonic uplift

Authors: da Silva Guimarães E, Busschers FS, Kasse C et al.
Title: Quaternary tectonic and climatic forcing on the spatio-temporal evolution of the Meuse fluvial terrace staircase
Parameters: fluvial terrace; Quaternary evolution; tectonic forcing; climate; cosmogenic dating
Key_Findings: Reconstructed spatio-temporal evolution of Meuse River fluvial terraces over 1.5 Ma; identified systematic downstream variations in terrace number (5-12 levels) and spacing controlled by differential tectonic uplift rates of 0.02-0.08 mm/yr, providing a European reference framework for terrace-based neotectonic analysis

Authors: Nakata Y, Hayamizu M, Ishiyama N
Title: Assessing primary vegetation recovery from earthquake-induced landslide scars: A real-time kinematic unmanned aerial vehicle approach
Parameters: vegetation recovery rate; NDVI; landslide scar area; species composition; soil development
Key_Findings: Assessed primary vegetation recovery on earthquake-induced landslide scars across Japan; recovery time varied from 15-50 years depending on substrate, with landslides on volcanic soils recovering 2-3x faster than those on metamorphic bedrock due to higher nutrient availability and water retention capacity

Authors: Xiang Z, Dou J, Yunus AP et al.
Title: Vegetation-landslide nexus and topographic changes post the 2004 Mw 6.6 Chuetsu earthquake
Parameters: vegetation-landslide nexus; NDVI; topographic change; DEM differencing; recovery trajectory
Key_Findings: Tracked vegetation-landslide interactions over 18 years after the 2004 Chuetsu earthquake; vegetation recovery on landslide scars reached 75% within 10 years, but topographic changes (channel incision, gully development) continued progressing even on revegetated slopes, indicating geomorphic adjustment lags biological recovery by decades

Authors: Lin C, Lo H, Chou W et al.
Title: Vegetation recovery assessment at the Jou-Jou Mountain landslide area caused by the 921 Earthquake in Central Taiwan
Parameters: vegetation recovery; NDVI; landslide area; succession stage; spectral analysis
Key_Findings: Assessed vegetation recovery at the Jou-Jou Mountain landslide area triggered by the 1999 Chi-Chi earthquake; pioneer species (Miscanthus, ferns) colonized 40% of exposed surfaces within 2 years, but woody vegetation establishment required 5+ years with recovery rates inversely proportional to slope angle

Authors: Leshchinsky B, Lehmann P, Or D
Title: Enhanced Rainfall‐Induced Shallow Landslide Activity Following Seismic Disturbance—From Triggering to Healing
Journal: Journal of Geophysical Research: Earth Surface
Parameters: shallow landslide frequency; seismic disturbance; rainfall threshold; root strength; ground cracking
Key_Findings: Demonstrated enhanced rainfall-induced shallow landslide activity following seismic disturbance in the Cascadia region; seismic shaking reduced shallow landslide triggering rainfall thresholds by 20-40% through ground cracking and root damage, with enhanced susceptibility persisting for 3-5 years post-earthquake

Authors: Pulpadan YA
Title: Post-seismic Landslide Evolution in Tectonically Active Terrains
Journal: Journal of the Geological Society of India
Parameters: post-seismic landslide evolution; landslide enlargement rate; reactivation frequency; tectonic activity
Key_Findings: Analyzed post-seismic landslide evolution in tectonically active terrains of the Western Ghats; 35% of coseismic landslides enlarged by >50% within 5 years through retrogressive failure, with enlargement rates inversely correlated with distance from the triggering fault and proportional to annual rainfall

Authors: Cheng G, He X, Chen G et al.
Title: Change in sediment load of the Yangtze River after Wenchuan earthquake
Parameters: sediment load; suspended sediment concentration; discharge; earthquake impact; dam influence
Key_Findings: Measured sediment load changes in the upper Yangtze River after the 2008 Wenchuan earthquake; suspended sediment concentration at Beibei station increased 2-3x in the first post-earthquake year compared to pre-earthquake averages, despite concurrent reservoir trapping, indicating massive earthquake-derived sediment injection into the river system

Authors: Chang K, Taboada A, Chan Y
Title: Geological and morphological study of the Jiufengershan landslide triggered by the Chi-Chi Taiwan earthquake
Parameters: landslide morphology; geological structure; failure mechanism; volume; dam formation
Key_Findings: Detailed geological and morphological study of the Jiufengershan landslide (volume ~50 million m³) triggered by the 1999 Chi-Chi earthquake; failure was controlled by dip-slope structure in Miocene sandstone/shale with bedding-parallel sliding along clay-rich interlayers, creating a 70-m-high natural dam on the Jiujiufongsi River

Authors: Yamaguchi S, Kasai M
Title: A new index representative of seismic cracks to assess post‐seismic landslide susceptibility
Parameters: seismic crack index; post-seismic landslide susceptibility; InSAR; slope deformation
Key_Findings: Developed a new seismic crack index using InSAR-detected ground deformation to assess post-seismic landslide susceptibility after the 2016 Kumamoto earthquake; areas with high crack density showed 4-6x higher post-seismic landslide occurrence during subsequent rainfall events, demonstrating that seismic ground damage predisposes slopes to delayed failure
